In my continuous learning journey, I am happy to share my recent achievements of MBA in Information Technology and Project Management with my LinkedIn connections. It was sheer a journey of two years, from January 2019 to May 2021. Time flies fast; after working full-time, I used to work on my assignments on weekdays, weekends occupied with research papers and project works, and twice a week - three to four hours of commute to school. It was challenging at first, but later on, I learn to manage my time wisely, prioritize tasks at best, and value time. 

I am thankful to Westcliff University, my employer ResMed for the support, and my Family for keeping faith in my perseverance in achieving my dreams.

P.S: I am so happy to enjoy my weekend back with no worries about completing an assignment.

This week I wrapped up my first days at Deque Systems, and it feels like the beginning of a chapter I’ve been moving toward for a long time.

After more than 17 years at Microsoft, I carry with me deep gratitude for the experiences, the people, and the impact we created together. That season shaped me and gave me countless opportunities to learn, grow, and contribute.

Now, at Deque, I feel that same sense of purpose expressed in a new way. The mission here — advancing digital equality and accessibility for all — is not just compelling, it’s lived daily. Even in my first week I’ve seen how deeply this commitment runs, and I’ve been inspired by the skill and dedication of the people I’ve met.

I’m excited to continue this journey, to bring what I’ve learned forward, and to add my voice to an organization so clearly aligned with values I hold close.
Here’s to new beginnings, built on a strong foundation.

interactions.original_post:
AI stopped being a cute copilot

1/ Top engineers report AI agents now write most of their production code, across web, backend, and even C.​
2/ New models (Opus 4.5, GPT‑5.2, Gemini 3) crossed an invisible line: suddenly, complex tasks that used to be painful are now “one‑shot” solvable.​

🧠 The bad news for “just coders”

1/ Being “the person who types fast in language X” is rapidly losing value; AI can jump stacks better than most humans.​
2/ Tasks like prototyping, refactoring, and implementing well‑defined tickets will be increasingly delegated to AI.​

🛠 The good news for engineers

1/ Clarifying what to build: breaking down vague ideas into precise behavior, edge cases, and constraints.​
2/ Owning quality: testing, CI/CD, observability, reliability, performance, security, tech debt.​
3/ Thinking like a product engineer: close to users, shipping fast, deciding what matters vs noise.​

AI writes more lines; humans own the outcomes.

😬 The ugly side

1/ More code → more bugs, more complexity, more infra cost; change failure rates are already going up in some orgs.​
2/ Mobile AI coding means “coding from your phone” becomes normal, and work–life boundaries risk being erased even more.​

💼 What this means for careers

1/ Juniors will be pushed to think like seniors earlier: architecture, testing, trade‑offs, not just syntax.​
2/ A solid CS / software engineering foundation may become a minimum bar, not a nice‑to‑have.​
3/ Demand for good software engineers might actually rise, as the amount of AI‑generated software explodes.​

Are you already letting AI write 50–90% of your code?
What skills are you actively doubling down on for the next 5 years?
#AI #SoftwareEngineering #DeveloperExperience #LLM #ProductEngineering

i went from charging $397 to $4,658/mo in 4 months.

same product.
nothing changed.
just charged 11 times more.
actually, it's less work now (rich people are less needy).

people always say "sell to the rich" but... 
nobody tells you HOW to actually do that.

so here's how i did it.

1. be more specific—use industry lingo.

you HAVE to talk like them.
non-negotiable.
the $397 client? i told them i could get them "lEaDs."
the $4,658 client? it was "strategic partnerships with R&D directors."

same thing.
different language.
stop saying "i can get you leads."
say "i can connect you with CFOs at mid-market PE firms."
or "i can book meetings with procurement directors at Fortune 500s."
industry lingo = you're speaking their language = premium pricing.

2. make deliverables LESS complicated.

the $397 client had to:

- sign up for 3 different softwares
- create logins
- join a slack workspace
- check a custom dashboard

the $4,658 client?

- i send them a text every 2 weeks.
- and we share a google sheet.

that's it.

no bullshit.
they pay premium to NOT deal with complications.
so i deal with it all.

3. find blue ocean markets—bring tech into OLD industries.

stop targeting mArkETing aGENciEs.
go after OLD MONEY.

wealth management.
biotech.
logistics.
investment banking.

these industries are still doing things the old way.

if you bring "modern" tech, you can charge a shit ton for simple stuff.

the $397 client was an accounting firm selling $5K packages.
the $4,658 client was biotech selling $6M contracts.

see the difference?

4. work with boomers.

i love working with boomers.
they're more patient.
they're blown away by the simplest advancements.
they're used to things taking weeks.

younger entrepreneurs expect FAST results.
boomers don't.

the $397 client was in his 30s.
the $4,658 client was in his 60s.

and guess who was easier to work with?

(the $397 client was still a pleasure to work with.)

ps. how much do you charge for your products?
interactions.user_text: Really appreciate this way of thinking.

🔥 “I didn’t write a single line of code this month.”

Boris Cherny, engineer 👨‍💻  at Anthropic and the creator of Claude Code, an AI-powered coding tool associated with the Claude family of model, recently tweeted such sentence. 

👉 What’s now a core developer tool started as a side project.
That’s amazing - and very telling.

IMHO with tools like Claude Code, Copilot, Cursor, etc., typing code is no longer the bottleneck.

 What’s becoming scarce is something else:
 • Clear intent
 • Good architecture
 • Strong constraints
 • High-quality review
 • Ownership of outcomes

When an AI “writes” the code, the engineer doesn’t disappear - they move up the stack.
You still:
▫️ decide what should exist
▫️ define how it should behave
▫️ review, reject, refine, and secure it
▫️ ensure it fits the product, the system, and reality

Saying “the AI wrote 100% of the code” is like saying a pilot didn’t fly because the autopilot was on. True in a narrow sense. Misleading in the one that matters.
The real change isn’t fewer engineers.
It’s engineers becoming directors, editors, and quality owners instead of typists. And this is still the beginning.
#SoftwareEngineering #AI #DevTools #ProductThinking #FutureOfWork #Claude #LLMs

Your biggest barrier to AI agent success is memory. Here’s what I’ve learned about giving AI the right context (after building multiple 7-figure ROI-generating AI solutions):

The biggest mistake enterprises make with AI is not starting.

After that, the biggest mistake is throwing all their data and context at one model, and expecting it to figure everything out.

It won’t.

1. Performance gets worse as you throw more information at a context window

2. Like humans, AI agents start to break down as you give them more varied tasks

3. Data’s often in different sizes and types, meaning you can’t apply one chunking strategy to everything equally

This poster is right - this is why long-term memory is the biggest limitation to agents.

But there’s a few things you can do to make agentic systems actually work with lots of data.

1. Distribute the duties. Instead of one agent to handle everything, break into specialized sub-agents to handle specific tasks in the workflow.

2. On system entry and before system exit, add fact-checking and security steps to make sure you’re getting the output you’re expecting, and large context windows aren’t causing the agent to lose control.

3. Give the agent as little to do as possible. Many systems we design only have AI for a small component, and use deterministic automations for the rest.

Does this solve the memory problem? Of course not. Not by itself.

Does it create AI solutions that generate real ROI for our customers? Of course it does.

That’s the difference.

I've built 50+ AI agents and every single one started with this simple blueprint...

Most people jump straight into building and wonder why their agent doesn't work.

The Agent Blueprint fixes this in 10 minutes:

- Forces you to define the actual problem (not just "automate stuff")
- Maps out exactly when and how it runs
- Identifies what data it needs access to
- Breaks the logic into clear steps
- Specifies where outputs should go

Once you fill it out, you can just describe it in plain English to an AI tool that builds the entire agent for you.

Perfect if you're non-technical.

No connecting nodes, no dragging boxes.

Just chat your way to a working agent.

The blueprint includes:

- The 5-question framework
- Full live tutorial showing me go from idea to working agent in under 10 minutes
- Cheat sheet with common trigger types, action verbs, data sources, output destinations
- 5 quick patterns you can steal

Connect with me + comment "AGENT" and I'll DM you the blueprint.

Here’s how I built an AI-powered inbox classifier using n8n

Step-by-step:
1️⃣ Trigger runs automatically whenever a new email is received
2️⃣ The workflow uses a custom text classifier trained on your labeled inboxes (e.g., Priority, Finance, Promotion, Clients, Internal)
3️⃣ It determines which category the email belongs to
4️⃣ Then it labels, routes, and even drafts a reply based on the context

This AI Inbox Classifier saves business owners hours every week sorting through endless messages — instantly flagging what’s important, and automating replies to routine emails.

You can easily connect it with Gmail, Slack, Notion, or any CRM, and have your AI assistant manage incoming messages for you in real time.

Quick question:

If you were to automate ONE part of your inbox, what would it be?
• support
• sales
• internal
• something else?

Comment "n8n" and I’ll send the workflow.
#n8n #AIautomation #EmailAutomation #WorkflowAutomation #Productivity #NoCode #Gmail #AIagents

interactions.original_post:
SEO isn’t just Google anymore, it’s “can assistants and agents find you?”

With a new website I'm digging into SEO and reminded just how long Google can take for visibility. 

However, in reality, a lot of buyers now ask ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini before they turn to Google and more teams are deploying AI agents (support, scheduling, sales ops) that need the same raw material to answer well.

Assistants and agent optimisation is now as important as SEO

• Make it findable, not fancy. Plain, public pages that answer real questions. FAQs, pricing ranges, locations, “who we’re for/not for.”

• Be quotable. Short, clear proof pages. Assistants love a clean source.

• Keep facts consistent everywhere. Site, LinkedIn, directories. Contradictions make assistants less confident in their recommendation.

• Write like the prompt. Use content the way people ask: “Best [service] for [problem] in [location]?” Then answer it straight.

• Document the process. Steps, SLAs, process stick when models summarise or agents explain decisions.

• Little and often. Fresh, factual updates compound faster than one perfect essay you never publish.

Does this surface faster than classic SEO?

Often yes for assistants and agents. Clear, crawlable, consistent content can be picked up in hours or days, meaningful Google movement is usually weeks or months. (Crawlability still matters for both)

Curious, has anyone had leads say, “we asked ChatGPT/Claude and your name came up” What did they see?

interactions.original_post:
There’s always a first time. 🙂
And every first brings its own emotions, excitement, energy, mistakes—and most importantly, learning.

Last week was full of firsts for me:
- My first time at the Microsoft offices in Seattle and the Bay Area
- My first global collaboration across Microsoft teams in three geographies
- My first time working with the investor ecosystem across India and the US in a single format
- And my first time being away from my 3.5-year-old daughter for 8–9 days💙

The highlight of the week: hosting a set of Indian VCs in our Bay Area office and discussing the future of tech and AI. Here are some of my personal reflections:

1. Shift in Microsoft’s DNA
Traditionally, Microsoft has been enterprise-first. But there’s a clear wave building - startups and investors are increasingly shaping how markets evolve and even how we build our products. The startup lens is no longer optional; it’s becoming core.

2. Investors as catalysts for AI in India
Interacting with leaders like Prayank Swaroop, Girish Mathrubootham (yes, a true fan-girl moment!), Lakshmi Shankar, Preeti S., Rahul Agarwalla, Brian Mullen, Krishna M., and Madhur Makkar reinforced a belief: investors are not just funding innovation, they are actively unlocking collaboration. For instance, many are thinking deeply about how their portfolio startups can leverage Microsoft’s distribution, GTM, and global reach.

3. Bridging ecosystems is hard but high-impact
One personal takeaway: as I double down on my role, I see how critical it is to make Microsoft easier to navigate for our investor and startup friends. The opportunity (and responsibility) is to simplify access, accelerate collaboration, and translate our vast ecosystem into tangible outcomes. Matti Suokko shared great learnings on this!

4. Global collaboration is powerful
Bringing together Microsoft's CoreAI leaders in the US with top Indian VCs created conversations that were candid, future-looking, and deeply energizing - from AI infrastructure to agents to scale-up stories. These interactions made it clear that innovation thrives when ecosystems cross borders.

This “first” is definitely one of many more to come. Excited to see how the future unlocks! 🚀

Puneet Chandok Nitin Mittal Kapil Chawla Lindsay Folk Mike Gaal Shaloo Garg Ayelet Steinitz William Tschumy Toni Hasson Berk Smita Roy Annie Pearl Himani Agrawal Micah Baldwin Shanoop Krishnan Rajat Malhotra Aashita Wason Nabyendu M. Vikram Upadhye Nidhi Hasija

I'm just back from our onsite with the whole Gorgias and it was one of the best weeks of my life! 

We celebrated the ten years of the company, it's been a hell of a ride growing the company from 0 to 16,000 brands with Alex Plugaru and the team. 

Now, we’re stepping into the next chapter. 

➡️ We believe conversations will become the primary way people shop. 

Conversational Commerce represents a market 10x larger than customer support software, and merchants are asking for one AI agent that can sell, support, and re-engage their customers seamlessly.

That’s what we’re building today:
- An AI agent that delivers quality, on-brand conversations.
- A seamless shopping experience where chat isn’t just for help, but for buying.
- Smart re-engagement that feels personal, not like another blast.

We are deeply saddened to confirm the death of one of our own, Spc. Sarah Beckstrom, a member of the West Virginia Army National Guard, who was shot in the line of duty near the Farragut Square Metro Station on November 26, 2025. Spc. Beckstrom was pronounced dead at MedStar Washington Hospital in the District of Columbia on November 27, 2025, as the result of wounds incurred during the shooting.

Spc. Beckstrom enlisted in the West Virginia National Guard on June 26, 2023, and served with distinction as a military police officer with the 863rd Military Police Company. She exemplified leadership, dedication, and professionalism.

Spc. Beckstrom, a 2023 graduate of Webster County High School, was a resident of Summersville, West Virginia. She volunteered to serve as part of Operation D.C. Safe and Beautiful helping to ensure the safety and security of our nation’s capital. Her loss is felt profoundly across our One Guard Family and throughout the Mountain State.

“On behalf of Governor Patrick Morrisey and the entire West Virginia National Guard, I extend my deepest condolences to Spc. Beckstrom’s family, friends, and fellow Guardsmen. We grieve alongside them and honor her memory by carrying forward her commitment to service, integrity, and excellence.” said Maj. Gen. Jim D. Seward, the Adjutant General of the West Virginia National Guard.

We ask that everyone respect the privacy of her family and her fellow Soldiers during this difficult time.

Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

This isn’t just about removing the standing power throw or renaming the ACFT. It’s about raising the bar for readiness, lethality, and shared standards.

Key changes:
• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run
• Sex-neutral, age-normed standards for 21 combat MOSs
• Min score: 350 total / 60 per event (combat MOSs)
• Min score: 300 total (combat-support MOSs)

This change reinforces our commitment to excellence - regardless of rank, MOS, or gender.

We lead with standards.

Are you ready to meet the challenge?

#ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #AJOC #ArmyStrong #JuniorOfficers #CombatReady #WarriorEthos #Lethality #Readiness 

The Army Leader Exchange  The Center for Junior Officers Kris Saling  US Army

"We want to hire top 0.1% engineers"

I'm hearing this a lot right now. 

But what does that actually mean any more?

Back in 2015 that was simple, it meant they had a Computer Science degree from a Top 10 global university, they had done internships at Meta / Palantir / Google / Slack / Dropbox etc  during their degree, they'd competed in Olympiads and that they worked at high bar tech company, FAANG / HFT / T1 Start ups etc. 

They could fizz buzz in their sleep, wrote clean performant code and could knock up a whiteboard design of Slack from first principles with minimal prompts.

I think it's different now.

When I’m speaking to founders they’re looking for things you can’t see on paper

🤝They want ownership
 🏃They want hustle
🧘They want low ego
😗👌They want product taste

They want to see entrepreneurial energy, they want to see engineers that vibe code side hustles on the weekend, that are prepared to throw themselves into something.  

The problem is, most companies don't know these abilities even when they're sitting in their inbox. The orthodoxy of hiring misses them all together.

This is the problem we are spending every moment obsessing over here Dex

Link in the comments to sign up...

Most modern AI boils down to matrix multiplications. Yet, fewer than 100 people worldwide have the practical experience to implement them efficiently across multiple platforms.

Since 2018, I've been working with tiled Matrix Multiplication instructions, starting with NVIDIA Titan Volta GPUs. It's frustrating to see how little things have improved. With custom instructions from CPU vendors — Intel Corporation AMX, Apple AMX, Arm SME, and more — each comes with its own complexities, making performance optimization harder, not easier.

It seems like CPU vendors are competing to create the most obscure Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) rather than focusing on Tensor Operations Per Second (TOPS). Take Intel AMX, for example — its 25-page documentation lacks details, like CPU support, compiler support, or even instruction latency/throughput!

At times, performance-oriented programming feels like hacking and reverse engineering!

---

If you're up for the challenge, check out the AMX manual and consider contributing to SimSIMD (

I built a solution to reduce cognitive load when working with multiple LLMs

The problem: You ask ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini the same question, then mentally synthesize three different answers.

The solution: "Merge mode" for PolyGPT

How it works:
 - Log into AI providers in split-screen (no API keys needed)
 - Ask your question once
 - All LLMs respond simultaneously
 - 4th window shows an AI-synthesized answer with citations [1] [2] [3]
 - Follow-up questions continue the merged conversation

Instead of reading three responses, you get one coherent answer combining the best of all models.

Inspired by Andrej Karpathy's "LLM council" concept, adapted for web-based LLMs.

Built on open source PolyGPT. Mac executable + code coming soon.

How do you handle comparing LLM responses?

#AI #LLM #ProductivityTools #OpenSource

90% of software engineers don't realize that they should be applying the Nvidia model to their own work.

Nvidia is a brilliant business as it doesn't care if individual AI plays succeed or fail. As long as there's some hitters, the space grows and they make money.

Instead of writing code to push a solitary feature that you own, write code that empowers other engineers to write code to push their awesome features.

This is a huge reason why so many engineers flee product teams to switch into infra teams, especially when pushing for Staff+ levels at Big Tech.

The colors of the family tree

Have you looked through old B&W pictures and wondered what the world looked like back then?

There's a certain resonance to shades of gray but once in a while it evokes curiosity.

The Singapore government worked on a project to convert old B&W images into color

They wanted to keep the texture and feel of the old pictures.

However, they're not using artists to do the job. This would not have been a must-do project

Apart from the cost and time this would entail, it was not practical to do manually.

So they used a bank of images to train the AI to work its magic on the palette

You can see the results, both the successful and the failed ones in the links below.

It worked best on high resolution pictures featuring humans and natural scenery.

Not the saturated, glossy and detailed millions of colors you see in images today.

May be that's for the best.

A colorful but slightly faded version of the world then

Not just in sepia

The project is now open for anyone to upload and experiment with their old B&W images

Are there family pictures you'd like to see in color?

#brands #behavioraleconomics #startups

As someone whose job at LinkedIn is to help seekers get hired, I feel compelled to write this post. Do *not* do this — do not put all your eggs in one basket (or 5).

I do not mean this as a criticism of Taylor's post: I think it's well intentioned & you're absolutely likely to increase your odds of landing a job if you spend time researching the company, making connections, etc (and that's what LinkedIn is great for!) However, job seeking is hard and there's a lot of variables that you wouldn't have accounted for.

Personal story: prior to LinkedIn, I was pretty close to joining Trello. The interviews had gone really well & I'd pretty much paused the rest of my job search. Then they first went cold before turning me down. No "we went with someone else". It's only later did I come to know that since Atlassian decided to shutter Hipchat, they had paused external hiring for a bit (to ensure they could move folks around internally).

It hurt to not get the role, but worse, I didn't have a pipeline. Please keep your options open at all times, no matter how well you think you're prepared & how well you've done in your interviews. Worst case scenario you'll have some leverage in negotiations. But keep searching until you've signed your offer & started your new role.

And all the best!
interactions.user_text: Moral of the story - do what works for you.

Xfinity (aka Comcast) has been causing so much trouble with my Internet connection. The price constantly goes up, and the quality regularly goes down. Today it was particularly terrible; I've been losing connection every 5 mins in the evening and every ~20 earlier during the day.

I know if I complain, they will use any potential reason to blame me for the issues (as it happened many times already): not their modem, not their router, connection to device not wired, etc. The only thing I didn't verify yet, is whether the connection was bad at the input to my Wi-Fi router, or my router is the problem (very unlikely, since it had worked fine for years). Hence, I decided to do that verification, too, before calling Xfinity.

I wanted a script to check the connection and log any interruptions. I will connect a machine with an ethernet cable directly to my modem and run that script for a day or two, and then check the logs for issues.

Long story short, I just want to show how useful the AI assistants (ChatGpt, Copilot) can be. Here's what I got in 2 mins; will start my testing tomorrow:

ingress-nginx is being retired. Best-effort maintenance ends at the end of this Month. After that, no releases, no bug fixes, and no security patches. Your deployments will keep running, but you'll be on your own. Any CVE discovered after retirement goes unpatched. If ingress-nginx is still handling your production traffic, that's a risk you can't afford to ignore.

In this talk, I'll share how we migrated from ingress-nginx to Istio across Bloomberg's large-scale multi-cluster Kubernetes platform built atop Karmada, without any downtime. I'll cover the incremental rollout strategy, the challenges of preserving routing semantics at scale, and the lessons learned along the way. If you're planning a similar migration, this one's for you.

📅 Tuesday, March 24 | 15:00 – 15:30 CET
📍 Hall 1–5 | Tram Zone | Cloud Native Theater
👉🏾 Register here: 

See you in Amsterdam! 👋🏾

#KubeCon #CloudNativeCon #Kubernetes #Istio #Nginx #Karmada #MultiCluster #CNCF

Confused by Kubernetes Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA)? I built a simple demo to clarify its mechanics.

Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) is now generally available in Kubernetes 1.35. However, with components like ResourceSlices, ResourceClaims, DeviceClasses, and CDI specs, it can be overwhelming. To simplify this, I created a basic example. A DRA driver that treats a file as a "device." 

This example uses no GPUs or FPGAs or network resources, just a file located at /etc/dra/file1. Why a file? It removes hardware complexity, allowing you to focus on understanding the DRA mechanics:

- How drivers publish devices via ResourceSlices
- How the scheduler matches pods to nodes
- How kubelet prepares devices through gRPC calls
- How CDI injects the device into containers

Once you grasp these patterns with a simple file, the mental model can easily transfer to real hardware like GPUs or Network devices.

The README includes architecture diagrams that explain the publish-subscribe pattern that drives DRA. 

You can check it out here: 

I hope this helps others better understand DRA.

🚀 llm-d v0.3.1 is Here: Now with ARM Support and Microsoft Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) Integration!

This latest patch builds on the momentum from our v0.3.0 release, delivering several key enhancements focused on expanding hardware compatibility, broadening cloud-native support, and improving the overall developer experience.

We're particularly excited about a few key updates:
💻 ARM Support: llm-d now supports ARM architectures! This is a significant step forward, enabling users to run and manage large language models on a wider, more diverse range of hardware.

☁️ AKS Cloud Provider Support: We've officially added Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) as a "well-lit" infrastructure provider. This makes it easier than ever for teams on Azure to deploy, manage, and scale their LLM workloads with llm-d.

🔧 Streamlined Image Builds: We've refactored our image build process into scripts and unified the GKE image into our core Cuda image. This simplifies maintenance, improves consistency, and makes the build process more transparent for our community.

This release also includes important bug fixes and stability improvements, thanks to the hard work of our dedicated team and community.

A special thank you to all our contributors, and a warm welcome to the many new faces who joined us for this release cycle. Your contributions are what make open-source projects like llm-d thrive.

For the full list of changes, bug fixes, and new contributors, check out the complete release notes on GitHub.

ACM has a new paper on #eBPF, which reiterates what the #Istio project has said for years about this technology: we'll adopt it when it's actually faster than user space.  

While certain specific applications saw substantial improvements, partial offload scenarios (such as mTLS and HTTP processing in a service mesh) saw no effect on latency and reduced overall throughput. eBPF is a powerful tool in specific scenarios, but can actually be harmful to your service mesh.

Why do people complain that Istio is complicated? It's just a handful of resources, 14 to be precise, including Telemetry, WasmPlugin and the old-school Gateway.

This is to certify that I (still) can type Istio config very fast. In a stressful and less than ideal settings of the exam platform.

On a serious note - I wish I'd done this before working on a large-scale enterprise Istio deployment. I do think Istio's architecture is clean and fairly straightforward. But I've also seen first-hand how wild it gets at scale in real world. What gets lost in the "Istio is too complex" debate is that much of that complexity was already there, the scale and the requirements. Additionally, in many cases Istio was adopted at a time when lot of the functionality which is table stakes today was not available back then.

interactions.original_post:
Today was my last day at Microsoft.

Closing this chapter is harder than I expected. The last 4.5 years weren’t just a role or a job—they were a journey of deep learning, meaningful ownership, hard problems, and even harder-earned trust. I grew alongside the product, built lasting bonds with incredible people, and had the privilege of shaping things at a scale I’ll always be proud of.
I’m profoundly grateful to a long list of leaders, peers, and teammates who believed in me, challenged me, and made this experience what it was.

To mark this moment, I decided to pause and reflect—on what this journey taught me, what surprised me, and what I’ll carry forward. I’ve captured those reflections in a blog post below. I am excited about what's next, and will share more soon.

🔗

interactions.original_post: I’m happy to share that I’m starting a new position as Member of Technical Staff at OpenAI!

I'm publishing a book---titled Last Branch Standing---with Penguin Random House that comes out April 14. Huge favor to ask: Would you please pre-order a copy today?  (Pre-orders are wildly important to the success of a book FYI!)

And what's the book about? As my publisher says, the book is "A myth-busting glimpse into the inner workings of the Supreme Court, revealing what we get wrong about the Roberts Court, what the justices' clerks gossip about, and how to fix a court in crisis." It's an explainer for non-lawyers with plenty of fun tidbits for lawyers too, including John Jay's head injury, why the end of the filibuster is bad for the judiciary (and law students!), and who on the Court is watching this season of Slow Horses.

I’m happy to share that I’ve joined a new team, in a newly created role, as AI Process Specialist at Microsoft! - We're delivering out-sized savings and operational efficiency to our internal customers using Microsoft's Artificial Intelligence tools (along with Power Platform and Fabric) within Continuous Improvement-centric projects.

Excited to be speaking at Istio Day during Kubecon NA this fall with Michael Bolot about the work we've been doing to build a service mesh atop Istio for databases! Istio doesn't just stop at microservices, and while it’s not easy, we've found there are a lot of benefits to running a Service Mesh across the stack. I look forward to sharing some of our design decisions and some of the hiccups we’ve hit along the way.

🌟 Community Spotlight 🌟
Shoutout to Keith Mattix II for his awesome contribution to agentgateway! 🚀

Keith introduced a stateful model configuration option for MCP backends that lets multiple gateway replicas handle requests interchangeably — all without needing shared session state.  This improvement boosts scalability, and simplifies architecture.

This kind of contribution not only strengthens agentgateway’s scalability and resilience, but also shows the power of our open-source community coming together to solve real-world challenges.

Thank you, Keith, for your work, collaboration, and commitment to making agentgateway better for everyone! 🚀

interactions.original_post:
I’m a free agent! ✨

Like many others this week, my team was eliminated and my role at Microsoft was impacted as part of the recent layoffs.

My thoughts and and support goes out to all of the other folks impacted by the layoffs this week. 

While this chapter is closing, I want to express deep gratitude to all the incredible people who made the last 3.5 years at Microsoft such a meaningful experience. It’s the people that made it special.

Despite the short run, I’m proud of the impact I made. I had the incredible opportunity to work directly with a variety of product groups and executives, contributing insights that led to changes and enhancements across AKS, Arc, and other Azure services.

Some other memorable highlights include collaborating on Learn Live sessions with cloud advocates, attending KubeCon, advising startups through the Microsoft for Startups program, being active in BAM, and appearing on Azure Friday a couple of times.

What I’ll take with me: growth, friendships, and unforgettable experiences working alongside some of the brightest minds in tech.

I’m not entirely sure where the next stop will be, but I know it will involve cloud, cloud native technologies, and some AI. Anyone who knows me knows I already have multiple irons in the fire. 

I enjoy leading high-impact teams and strategic initiatives. If you’re hiring for roles in product management, building AI solutions, multi-cloud, cloud native, GitOps, or platform engineering, I’d love to connect and explore how I can help drive impact at your organization. 

I’m open to new opportunities and my DMs are open. 

---------------
Special shoutout to the talented product managers and engineers I had the honor of leading during my time at Microsoft. 📢 

These folks were also impacted by the layoffs and are tech rockstars. I'd work with them again in a heartbeat. Please connect with them and reach out if you have roles:

Michael Papasevastos
Danial Shakeri
Javier Rodriguez
Avinash Patney

Please like and share this post for maximum reach. Thanks in advance!

#OpenToWork #Microsoft #Azure #Cloud #CloudNative #AI #Multicloud #OpenSource #SaaS #Startups #Leadership #BAM

Well it’s one of those posts. Today is my last day at Microsoft. It’s been my home for the past 8 or so years. I still remember the phone call from the recruiter asking if I wanted to join to help Microsoft with Open Source and thinking the guy was insane asking a die hard OSS person to join “the enemy” as I’d seen them over the years. I couldn’t have been more wrong. It’s been a privilege to work with so many wonderful people as a Global Black Belt, as a Business Lead in the UK subsidiary and as a Product lead in the AKS team at HQ. Thank you to my team, my managers over the years and to the hundreds of people I’ve worked with on great projects at Microsoft and the Kuberbetes community.

🌐 If Kubernetes was designed in 2025, would we have zero-trust networking enabled by default for the data plane 🤔 ?

I posit 𝐍𝐎𝐓. In one word - DevEx and in two words - Developer Experience 🤖. But there is still hope, keep on reading 😃 !

The 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐧-𝐛𝐲-𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐚𝐮𝐥𝐭 𝐧𝐞𝐭𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐦𝐨𝐝𝐞𝐥 in Kubernetes was an 𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐚𝐥 𝐝𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧 𝐜𝐡𝐨𝐢𝐜𝐞 (rather than an afterthought) to encourage adoption and reduce friction:
1️⃣ 𝐋𝐢𝐟𝐭-𝐧-𝐬𝐡𝐢𝐟𝐭 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐥𝐨𝐚𝐝𝐬 (𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐤𝐧𝐨𝐰𝐥𝐞𝐝𝐠𝐞) - In a VM world, networking is typically open by default inside a data center
2️⃣ 𝐄𝐧𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐫𝐚𝐠𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐫𝐚𝐩𝐢𝐝 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐭𝐨𝐭𝐲𝐩𝐢𝐧𝐠 - if security policies were too restrictive by default, it would make deploying multiple apps too complex and would kill the adoption
3️⃣ 𝐒𝐞𝐜𝐮𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐢𝐬 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐨𝐧𝐞-𝐬𝐢𝐳𝐞-𝐟𝐢𝐭𝐬-𝐚𝐥𝐥 - the risk appetite, certificates and secrets compliance and governance varies across different industries and organizations

Docker, Inc won the development war for containers providing a super intuitive and straightforward CLI and ecosystem.
Adding 𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐚𝐮𝐥𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐲 𝐫𝐮𝐥𝐞𝐬 and/or 𝐢𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐲-𝐛𝐚𝐬𝐞𝐝 𝐧𝐞𝐭𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 to Kubernetes would have been 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐥𝐚𝐬𝐭 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐥 𝐢𝐧 𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐜𝐨𝐟𝐟𝐢𝐧 against Docker Swarm or Mesos for winning the production war for container orchestration and deployment.

Remember Pod Security Policies (PSP) and its evolution to Pod Security Standards (PSS)?
I envision something similar for Kubernetes networking as well.

👉 Here's an alternative design idea (2025 Kubernetes):
Instead of fully open-by-default, Kubernetes could adopt Cloud VM-style networking with different networking profiles, consider this for a baseline:
* Default allow for same-namespace communication (like a VM subnet)
* Default deny for cross-namespace communication (like VPC firewall rules)

And:
* An add-on for mTLS (in the spirit of Gateway API et al.) - via kubectl apply -f

🫥 In 2025, even with all the great OSS/paid solutions out there, zero-trust networking still feels super low-level and we need to know about the existence and high-level mechanics of CNIs / Service Meshes, or policy engines / CEL rules that would generate default deny network policies.

🔮 What do you think? Should Kubernetes introduce built-in security profiles for networking, similar to how Pod Security Standards (PSS) replaced PSP?

#kubernetes #networking #k8s #cni #servicemesh #servicemeshes #kubernetesnetworking #signetwork #cloudnative
interactions.user_text: Ofir Cohen yes I believe it will be!

🚀 Big news as we closed out Microsoft Ignite last week!

We’re thrilled to announce Observability in Foundry Control Plane, a game-changer for developers building and managing AI agents at scale.

Why does this matter? AI agents are powerful—but unpredictable. Ensuring trust, safety, and performance requires deep visibility across their lifecycle. 

That’s where Observability in the Microsoft Foundry comes in with the following capabilities:

✅ Integrated & Continuous Evaluations – Out-of-the-box and custom evaluators, synthetic datasets, and cluster analysis to accelerate reliable agent development.
✅ Unified Monitoring Dashboards – Track cost, performance, and safety with actionable insights.
✅ End-to-End Tracing – OpenTelemetry-based tracing for debugging every agent run.
✅ Fleet-Wide Oversight – Observe agents across Foundry and third-party frameworks in one view.
✅ AI Red Teaming Agent – Automate safety testing to harden generative AI systems.

CarMax joined us on stage to share how these capabilities help them monitor and optimize their AI agent, Skye—boosting confidence and accelerating innovation.

With these tools, organizations can evaluate, monitor, and optimize every agent responsibly and efficiently—from prototype to production.

👉 Dive deeper into the details: Read the full blog post: 

#MicrosoftIgnite #AzureAI #MicrosoftFoundry #Observability #AIInnovation

🚀 VoteMate Preview is live! → votemate.io

This moment has been months in the making, and it only happened because of the incredible people I’ve been lucky enough to build with.

Change doesn’t happen alone - it takes a group of people willing to show up, put in the work, and believe in something bigger than themselves. Every late night, every brainstorming session, every line of code, every design sketch - it all came from this volunteer team. And we wouldn’t be here without them.

In just 10 months, together we’ve:
-Built VoteMate with 50+ volunteers and Responsible AI leaders from Microsoft and elsewhere, supported by Microsoft for Startups.
-Collaborated with Carnegie Mellon University - Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy on a Summer R&D program and Fall Capstone.
-Established partnerships with trusted civic nonprofits like Democracy Works and Vote Early Day.
-Secured Patent Pending status on our personalized ballot-building AI system.
-Brought a Preview product to life on minimal funding and zero debt - driven entirely by the heart and hustle of volunteers.

This Preview release is an early step - focused first on New Jersey and Virginia for upcoming elections - but it’s proof of what’s possible when people across backgrounds, professions, and beliefs come together around a shared purpose.

How you can support:
- Explore the platform at votemate.io
- Share it with friends, family, your network
- Send us your feedback through the app - every bit helps us grow!

I want to take a moment to celebrate and thank every single person who got us here. Words are not enough to express my gratitude!

Some Thoughts on Development

As a developer, I care a lot about the scalability of the systems I build and like to think long-term.

But that’s not always the case. Sometimes, I realize I just need to kick it off and get it done. There are times when I don’t need to over-engineer the code or create long-term solutions. Real-world problems can be quite different from ideal system designs.

For instance, if we’re building a proof of concept, it might be more important to deliver quickly than to ensure the system can handle millions of users. We can always refine and scale it later if the concept proves successful.

interactions.original_post:
Well, we did it.

This morning, as the sun was coming up, we completed the commercialization for SUNDAYS, a low carb and zero sugar Oreo I've been developing since early 2020.

In case anyone reading this wants to follow in my footsteps, here's the TL;DR on what it took;

32 months from start to finish

3 different co-packers in 2 different countries

3 separate R&D teams

5 full scale, production size trials

Hundreds of hours of bench work

Innumerable formula iterations

The total development cost was somewhere around $200,000

For one second today, it felt like I got to the finish line.

Then I remembered that I'm not at the finish line.

I'm at the starting line.

SUNDAYS will launch later this year.

Date is TBD.

Beyond grateful to everyone here that's been following along.

It's day zero.

Let's go baby!!

interactions.original_post:
📢 📢 Do you want to learn how to use Azure Firewall's 🛡 Auto-learn SNAT routes feature? This feature can help you optimize your network configuration in Azure by automatically learning the out-of-network address spaces from the Azure Route Server and applying these as non SNAT routes in the Azure Firewall Policy. In this blog, we will show you how to enable this feature in the Azure Portal, and how it works with your network virtual appliance and the Azure Route Server. You will also see the benefits of using this feature, and how it can save you time and effort in defining the SNAT private ranges. Auto-learn SNAT routes is a public preview feature that can enhance your network experience in Azure, so don't miss this opportunity and give it a try today! 📢 📢 

⭐ Special shout out to Daniel Mauser and his amazing NVA repos and walk throughs. If you'd like to use the same appliance as I did to build out my NVA in Azure as part of a POC, you can find his GitHub repos in the reference section of the blog. ⭐

This week I said goodbye to a people that made work feel like home.
Not the building, not the badge — the people.
The ones who sat beside me through deadlines, laughed with me on the hard days, and clapped louder for my wins than I did for myself.

There’s a picture of them holding a sign saying “Go crush it” — and it’s more than a send-off.

Covid-19 and Air Travel

The airlines mandate a RT-PCR test before boarding a flight. The test can be done at most 72 hours prior to flight.

This mandate does not rule out the possibility of Covid transmission completely.

I got Covid positive while coming from New Delhi to San Francisco.  I am not 100% sure from where did I get it. But my Covid Test on second day after arrival was negative. 

My symptoms appeared after four days and second Covid test on 5th day after arrival came positive. Unfortunately, the whole family was affected by that time. 

I did not see any news about my flight, but a quick web search showed similar incidents:





My learnings:
- Isolate and quarantine for at least four days after travel
- Do not rush for a Covid test without symptoms
- PPE kit in the flight may decrease your chance for infection

Stay safe!

I wrote my first computer program sometime back in year 2011. Fascinated I decided to pursue a career path that would let me program to my heart's content. The environment in my undergrad, #BITSPilani, was conducive of my ambition, and I couldn’t be more grateful. Long story short almost eight years later tomorrow I’m going to start  as a Software Developer at #Microsoft, Redmond with emphasis on #BigData & #DataEngineering in their Business Application Organization. 

In this story I’ve rounded off the edges, cut out my lucky breaks and left out the days of self-doubt. It hasn’t been a smooth journey. Then, I didn’t expect it to be either - there is no triumph without toll.  I’m thankful for the struggles I experienced, and don’t regret any of my decisions. My family and friends have been a constant source of support. I cannot emphasize enough the role they’ve played to help me achieve this milestone.

#BITSian #Microsoft #Job #Redmond #LearnerForLife

interactions.original_post:
I saw many engineers blaming the outage on Microsoft 🤦‍♂️ SWEs blaming without knowing the root cause is concerning.

It is not Microsoft, it is Crowdstrike who released an update for Windows that had a bug. The patch runs in Kernel mode to monitor system activity at a low level.

Because it was running in Kernel mode, the buggy code was trying to access an invalid memory location that triggered a panic and showed a Blue Screen of Death.

The name of the driver file that had the buggy update is "C-00000291.sys", deleting it fixes the issue, and unfortunately, this needs to be done manually.
 
Microsoft has nothing to do with it.

Koo an indigenous Bharatiya company & Twitter alternative is scheduled to shut down. 

But this isn't Bharat's first loss. 

Hike Messenger was launched in 2012 to rival WhatsApp. 

It was way ahead of its time and had better features than WhatsApp. 

-) Offline Messaging: First of its kind, WhatsApp doesn't have this even in 2024.
-) Video Calling: Hike launched it in September 2016, whereas WhatsApp launched in November 2016.
-) Voice Calling: Hike- January 2015, WhatsApp- March 2015.
-) Hike Direct: Launched in October 2015, this feature allowed users to share files without an internet connection using Wi-Fi Direct. WhatsApp still does not have an equivalent feature.
-) Hidden Mode: Hike has had this feature since 2014, WhatsApp introduced it 10 years later and still doesn't provide any encryption.
-) Stickers: Hike was one of the first messaging apps to introduce various stickers, including regional ones.
-) Timeline and Stories: Hike had its version of Stories and Timeline updates before WhatsApp introduced the Status feature in February 2017.

Hike died because Indians have an inherent bias towards foreign products. 

We suffer from colonial hangover and a Made in USA product implicitly gets preferred over a Made in India product. 

According to me, Hike Messenger's death is one of our greatest losses in the product space. 

We had a mammoth opportunity to tackle Meta and we lost it. 

Agree?

#hike #whatsapp #koo

🚦 Exciting Milestone at Soham System Solutions! 🚦

With the blessings of Lord Ganesha, my parents, and elders, I’m happy to share that Soham System Solutions has officially stepped into product innovation with the launch of our SigWiz suite of traffic management products.
Traffic management is a global challenge. When we looked closely, we realized traditional traffic equipment is often complicated to set up, operate, and maintain. That’s where we saw an opportunity — to design products that are simpler, smarter, and more reliable.
This journey wasn’t easy. Along the way, we:

• Built a world-class team in software, PCB design, video analytics, industrial design, and manufacturing
• Developed a strong supplier ecosystem delivering on-time, high-quality components
• Created innovative, patent-pending solutions tailored for real-world challenges

We are now ready to collaborate with governments, municipalities, civil contractors, infrastructure companies, technology partners — and anyone looking to improve traffic management with robust, scalable solutions.

Excited to bring you a new blog: "Microsoft PostgreSQL OSS engine team: reflecting on 2024" (

In this first part of a two part blog, you will learn about who the Microsoft PostgreSQL OSS Engine team is, their code contributions to upstream PostgreSQL, technical details behind those changes & their journey during 2024. 

In the second part which is coming soon, you will get a sneak preview of upcoming work in 2025 (and PG18 cycle) and more.

Thank you to the team: Andres Freund, Amit Langote, Ashutosh Bapat,  Daniel Gustafsson, David Rowley, Melanie Plageman, Mustafa Melih Mutlu, Nazır Bilal Yavuz, Rahila Syed, Thomas Munro & Tomas Vondra for all the  hard work and dedication you bring to PostgreSQL. Thank you to the amazing PostgreSQL development community for the great work, which makes PostgreSQL the best and the most loved database in the world. 

Thank you Claire Giordano, Teresa Giacomini for helping me polish the blog contents and Guy Bowerman for the final push to publish.

See you all soon with part 2: "Microsoft Postgres OSS engine team: previewing 2025" !

InformationWeek published an article on the future of cybersecurity and the impact of quantum computers.  The article asks some very relevant questions, including:
* How vulnerable organizations will become if they do not make such a transition (to post-quantum cryptography) fast enough? 
* Will it be open season on their data without quantum encryption? 
* Does the post-quantum future mean quantum encryption will be mandatory for even the most basic forms of cyber resilience?

Min-Hank Ho, our VP of Product Management, provides his perspective on these questions in the article

Acest documentar de două ore, care comprimă o muncă de investigație de peste un an și jumătate, încearcă să explice mecanismele complicate prin care sistemul judiciar, un pilon cheie al oricărui stat democratic, a ajuns să fie capturat de un grup de interese format din magistrați și politicieni.

Într-o formă simplificată, pactul dintre cele două tabere arată așa: politicul a oferit legi care au creat o organizare piramidală a sistemului de justiție, punând toată puterea în mâinile unui grup restrâns de magistrați, iar această mână de magistrați a oferit la schimb o justiție care nu-i mai deranjează pe cei puternici.

Mărturiile pe care le-am adunat din interiorul sistemului de justiție și pe care le prezentăm în acest material sunt de o gravitate fără precedent. Ele vorbesc despre dosare de mare corupție în care judecătorilor li se sugerează să ia decizii favorabile inculpaților, despre încălcarea flagrantă a principiului repartizării aleatorii a dosarelor prin schimbarea discreționară a completurilor de judecată, despre cum magistrații care nu se supun sunt marginalizați, hăituiți prin anchete ale Inspecției Judiciare și uneori chiar excluși din magistratură.

Consecințele acestor realități dramatice ne afectează viețile tuturor, pentru că o societate democratică nu poate supraviețui fără o justiție independentă:

Is this the end of the 5-day week? 👀
 
 
The results are in and the report tells us that the UK’s four-day week pilot was a huge success! 🎉
 
Involving 61 companies and around 2,900 workers, this is the world’s largest four-day week trial to date. 
 
 Quick facts:

🤸‍♂️ 92% of the participating employers are sticking with the four-day week
 
 🤸‍♂️ Company revenue rose by 1.4% on average
 
 🤸‍♂️ 96% of workers prefer the four-day week
 
 🤸‍♂️ Employee stress levels dropped by 39%
 
 🤸‍♂️ Employee burnout rates fell by 71%
 
 🤸‍♂️ 54% of workers felt a reduction in negative emotions
 
 🤸‍♂️60% of employees with care responsibilities saw an improvement to their work-life balance, and 62% reported a positive impact to their social lives
 
 🤸‍♂️ 48% of employees feel more satisfied than when they first started
 
 🤸‍♂️ Sick days plummeted by 65%
 
 🤸‍♂️ Staff resignations dropped by 57%

By the end of the trial, 46% of workers said they’d only accept a five-day week job if they received a pay increase, and 15% said that no amount of money could entice them return to a five-day schedule.
 
Hard as some will most definitely try, you simply can’t argue with those stats! And for those who do wish to air their concerns, there are a couple of things worth noting first:
 
 👉 One size doesn’t fit all, which is why participating firms designed a policy tailored to factors such as their industry, organisational challenges, departmental structures, and company culture.
 
 This means a variety of four-day weeks were created, from the classic ‘Friday off’ model, to alternative structures like ‘staggered’, ‘decentralised’, ‘annualised’, and ‘conditional’.
 
 👉 To avoid any biased data, companies who took part come from a range of sectors and vary in size and headcount. 
 
So, what’s your verdict? Does today mark the beginning of the end of the five-day work week? Let us know your thoughts below, and be sure to click the link in the comments to read the full report! 👇

#flexacareers #fourdayweek #4dayworkweek #employmenttrends #hr #data #work #hiring #recruitment #culture #

Don’t fall for false DeepSeek R1 news! Deepseek R1 made it to mainstream, but there are so many wrong takes and false information. ℹ️

> No, the training didn’t cost only ~$6M dollars; the compute for the base model (no RL) was equivalent to GPU hours worth $5.5M, excluding ablations, smaller runs, data generation, and any of DeepSeek R1 training.

> No, it's not a side project (maybe started as one). DeepSeek is backed and owned by High-Flyer, a Chinese hedge fund; in 2020, they managed assets of over 7 billion dollars, and their talent includes Olympic medalists in mathematics, physics, and informatics.

> No, they don’t just have a few GPUs. They have ~50k GPUs.

> The real Deepseek R1 is 671B MoE model that needs > 16x 80GB Memory (16x H100s).

> Yes, the Deepseek R1 671B is really good! And they do great open source and science work > 2 years already

> There are 6 “distilled” versions. They are fine-tuned Qwen and Llama on 800k samples, (NO RL). Thats not “r1”. The smallest one with 1.5B (Yes, you can run locally, but it’s not near R1).

> Yes, the hosted version on chat(.)deepseek(.)com might use your data to train new models (as per ToS of DS).

> Yes, Open Science and Source will benefit everyone in the long term! Hugging Face is working on a fully open reproduction pipeline

If you are curious how Deepseek R1 trained, I wrote a blog:

🚀 No more sharing messy Prompts on Slack with your team!

POML (Prompt-Oriented Markup Language) might be the next big unlock in LLMs.

Today, prompts are free-form text. Great for humans, painful for infra.
Enter POML → a structured way to define prompts with components, reusability, and observability.

Why it matters:
	1.	Easier debugging + logging
	2.	Caching + optimization become real
	3.	Prompts portable across models

Infra made compute efficient.
POML can make intelligence efficient.

#AIInfrastructure #LLM #POML  Microsoft

I walked out of Intel Jones Farm campus earlier today with mixed feelings. Like many other folks, I opted into getting the Enhanced Retirement Package. Today, 9/30/2024, is my last day at Intel Corporation.

I started working at Intel 29 years ago. It was an incredible journey. I had the privilege of knowing, meeting, learning from, and working with amazing and talented folks at Intel.

Thank you, Intel!

I look forward to embarking on a new endeavor.

My last day at Intel after 31 years (!!) was August 2nd. What a wild and wonderful ride! I'm feeling a bit bittersweet, but looking forward to my next job, starting in a couple of weeks (stay tuned!).

Throughout my career at Intel, I've had the opportunity to wear many hats. While my primary focus was on networking drivers, my journey took a few interesting detours along the way. Working for a big company always felt comfortable to me, and I cherished being part of a team that changed the world through technology. My niche was networking—primarily ethernet, and eventually, a lot of Linux.

Though I enjoyed the stability and the impact of working at a large company, some of my most memorable moments came from working on smaller, more focused teams. That sense of close-knit collaboration and singular focus is something I'm eager to experience again in my next chapter.

I've met countless incredible people at Intel and throughout the industry. I am deeply grateful for the lessons (both good and bad!) that they've taught me. Our lives are made from our experiences, and I'm thankful for all of them—though some I might not choose to repeat! 😄

Thank you, Intel, for an unforgettable 31 years. I suspect I'll always have blue blood from working so long at Intel! Here's to new adventures and myself finally taking that leap I've watched so many colleagues take! #ThankYou #NewBeginnings #Networking

The competition for Day 0 or even Day -1 slots in Indian campus recruitment is becoming intense, bordering on craziness.
Companies want to secure slots earlier than others and are offering incentives to colleges to do so. Colleges, in turn, are looking to capitalize on this situation by improving their recruitment records or securing sponsorships for college fests.
However, in this process, two important participants—the interviewers and the candidates—are being blatantly ignored. Recruitment is starting at midnight, often going into the early hours of the morning. Despite all recommendations and research, companies expect interviewers and candidates to make sharp decisions at 2 or 3 a.m., when they are sleep-deprived.
What would be lost if interviews started in the morning? Why have we come to accept this as normal?

'Joy of Reading' needs your help

“Reading opens an infinite world of ideas and sparks imagination in children all over the world. Gift a book and spread the joy of reading.”

This year we are enrolled in ‘Benevity Causes Portal’ which enables donations through your company’s giving portals. During this giving month, I request you to consider donating to 'Joy of Reading' and help us spread the Joy and fulfill our mission of “Enabling every child to immerse in the world of Books."

More about our work: At Joy of Reading ( we tailor our contributions to the specific needs of government schools. By supplying books that cater to the unique demographics of each school, we ensure that every child has access to literature that resonates with them. Beyond just providing books, we share our expertise on library setup, maintenance, and management. We also train librarians on their pivotal roles and effective methods to foster reading among children. Additionally, we offer insights on conducting children's reading level assessments, advise on executing a structured library hour, guide English teachers on utilizing the library as a teaching resource, and provide strategies to support struggling readers. Through these efforts, we empower government school teachers to manage libraries efficiently, cultivate reading habits in children, and assist those who face reading challenges.

Got my 5 year anniversary crystal today.

While choosing the crystal, I was musing over whether it would be practical to just take the cash reward. It felt a bit silly to attach so much value to an object. Aren’t moments and experiences supposed to matter more than souvenirs?

Fast forward to unboxing the crystal today, and it sent me straight down memory lane. Building the Connections app from scratch, the 1.5M celebration, singing harmonies at the gala evening, so many team outings, and now contributing to next‑gen SharePoint – the past 5 years have been full of learning and fun moments. I’ve been lucky to be part of a team that celebrates each other so much.

Huge gratitude to Amit Dhodapkar, Ashish Singhal, Snehdip Karandikar, Venu Sanda, Vinit Baheti and my entire team for these last 5 years.

Glad I chose the crystal – I've realized that it is not just a keepsake, rather a physical reminder of five years of work, growth and memories. Almost like a little memoire sitting on my desk.
Looking forward to what’s next!

🚀 𝗜 𝗮𝗺 𝘀𝘂𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗲𝘅𝗰𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗮𝗻𝗻𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗜 𝘄𝗶𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝗲 𝗷𝗼𝗶𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 #𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗥𝗼𝗯𝗼𝘁 𝗮𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗩𝗣 𝗼𝗳 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝘁 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺 𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗺.

🔙 𝗟𝗼𝗼𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸....
... when I joined UiPath, it had an amazing technology that enterprises were excited about because it held immense promise to transform how they operate in positive disruptive ways. To unlock that promise, we needed to make UiPath enterprise-ready, scalable, and cloud-native.

Over the past 7 years, we went well beyond that, we delivered the most comprehensive enterprise governance and automation capabilities in the market in a way that supported not just RPA, but a unified suite of products working together as part of an integrated platform, trusted by some of the most complex and regulated enterprises and public sector globally.

The recent release of UiPath Act2 agentic capabilities directly integrated into the platform is a milestone that captures the flexibility and power of the UiPath platform, enabling customers to manage agents just like robots, but also accelerating the pace at which new innovations can be delivered internally in UiPath as well as for customers.

None of this would have been possible without the brilliant, and collaborative people I had the privilege of working with across multiple teams and disciplines. Thank you to everyone who leaned in, stood up for the customer, challenged and listened, and helped turn vision into execution.

A special shout-out, and, many, many, many thanks to the platform team, the engine behind the scenes that makes things "just work". Success for us was making things invisible :), ensuring everything felt seamless, integrated, and frictionless for the user while offering the control and governance enterprises expect. When it all just worked, it meant we did our job right.
🙏 It was a privilege and a pleasure working with you all.

Today marks 25 years at Microsoft — a journey that’s been both humbling and deeply fulfilling.  What started as an opportunity to work on personal finance products turned into a lifelong journey of learning, building, and growing across continents, technologies, and people.

Over these years, I’ve had the privilege of contributing to products that touched millions from Financial Products to MSN Core Platform, Bing Search, Microsoft AI, and now Azure Cloud. Along the way, I’ve been part of teams that shaped the evolution of the web, cloud, and AI platforms that define Microsoft today.

My journey has taken me from Redmond to India, with stops in Japan and Singapore. Through this journey, I’ve had the privilege to work with incredible people - colleagues, mentors, and partners, who inspired me every single day.

I’ve seen Microsoft reinvent itself many times and through each reinvention, I’ve grown too. The constant has been the people: curious, passionate, and deeply collaborative, which is why Microsoft remains such a special place for me!

I’m deeply grateful for the opportunities, the learning, and the trust placed in me through the years.  Thank you to each person that’s been part of my journey - each of you have helped shape me and for that I’m always grateful. Wishing each of you well wherever you are! Here’s to the next chapter of growth, innovation, and purpose. 💙

#Microsoft #25Years #Gratitude #Leadership #Azure #AI #Engineering

After more than 15 years at Microsoft, I made the leap from corporate leadership into full-time entrepreneurship — and what a journey it has been!
I’m proud to share that over the last few years, I’ve transitioned from building global cloud infrastructure systems to building businesses that serve families, communities, and investors.

What started as a career shift has turned into a passion for scaling businesses, developing teams, and giving back to the community. Along the way, our companies have raised tens of thousands for causes like Make-A-Wish and Best Buddies — a reminder that success is best when it’s shared.

Excited to share that I’ve officially completed my Master’s in Computer Science from Georgia Institute of Technology, with a specialization in Interactive Intelligence 🎓💡.

Over the past three years, I explored a wide range of subjects like Machine Learning, Natural Language Processing, AI for Robotics, Video Game Design, AI etc. From experimenting with algorithms to training (sometimes endlessly, sometimes successfully 😅), building models, writing reports to building video games. Every course pushed me to think creatively while honing both my technical skills and problem-solving mindset.

Balancing a demanding graduate program with full-time work wasn’t always easy - late-night projects, weekend sprints, and rolling straight into Monday meetings, but it was absolutely worth it. I’m especially grateful to my parents, GaTech faculty & peers and my managers - Saradhi, Ramesh and Karan for their support throughout.

Looking forward to applying these skills to build innovative solutions and take on exciting, real-world challenges.

Here’s to the next chapter and all the opportunities it brings! 🚀

📍 Does “Selling For Too Little” Even Exist?
+ Top VC Funds and Accelerators for Startup Founders

Critics love to talk about the startups that “only” sold for a million dollars. They love to belittle the efforts of founders without ever having been a founder.

As discouraging as their opinions can be, consider:
🔻 They aren’t the founder. You are. It’s easy to criticize from the sidelines, but they have no right to do so.
🔻 “Life-changing” is enough. Whether you’ve just made $2 million or $200 million, you’ve still completely altered your life for the better.
🔻Selling is always a win. The fact that you’ve reached this milestone is worthy of celebration, regardless of the particulars of the sale.

Tune out the critics. Instead, focus on your incredible accomplishment.

To help you reach that milestone sooner, I’ve compiled a list of VC funds and accelerators for startup founders. Use this list to make your next move, and get one step closer to that sale.

A quick reminder for anyone who might need it:
(We all reach that point sometimes)

When you’re constantly drained, it shows in everything.
→ Your creativity fades.
→ Your productivity slows.
→ You become irritable, exhausted, and mentally worn out.

That’s not just pressure.
That’s the early stage of burnout.
Which is why protecting your energy is essential.

Here are a few ways to do it:
1) Create Clear Work Limits
↳ End your workday when it’s supposed to end.
↳ Don’t allow work to take over your evenings or weekends.

2) Protect Time Between Tasks
↳ Leave 10–15 minutes between meetings.
↳ Step away, breathe, and reset your focus.

3) Review Your Workload Each Week
↳ Be honest about how much you’re carrying.
↳ Identify pressure early before it becomes overwhelming.

4) Learn to Decline Respectfully
↳ Your time is valuable.
↳ If something isn’t critical, postpone it or pass it on.

5) Avoid Taking On Too Much
↳ You’re not expected to handle everything.
↳ Focus on doing your role well instead of doing everything.

6) Make Rest a Priority
↳ You’re human, not a machine.
↳ Quality sleep and mental downtime matter.

You can’t perform at your best when you’re running on fumes.
Pause.
Recover.

Realign.
Taking care of yourself isn’t a luxury.
It’s essential.
What are your thoughts?

♻️ If this resonated with you, share it with your network.

I know, this is not great content for a Monday morning, 

especially, because this is about a layoff situation! :-)

But hear me out.

You may enjoy this scene as much as I did, and you may have a great takeaway!

George Clooney, a specialist in "employment termination"

Basically, a guy who tells the employees that they have been laid off, probably in a nice and positive tone (whether or not the receiver feels the same!)

We all start working to make a living, but eventually, we end up living our entire life that way, which is such an unfortunate thing!

We all can do a bit of planning and help ourselves to lead a better and bigger life, over time, gradually, with some sincere effort!

What do you think?

The scene is from the movie, Up In The Air!

While onsite at a client office today, I was inspired by their community service event held in the office.

🌟 Community = Growth.

One of the things I’ve grown to appreciate most in my career—especially in tech—is the incredible impact of community. Not just the communities we work in every day, but the ones we build, support, and invest in beyond our desks.

🤝 Whether it’s partnering with local universities (an effort I have found to be one of the most rewarding), mentoring folks that are early in their careers, volunteering at community events, or simply sharing with others what we’ve learned, outreach and connection isn’t a side project. It’s part of who we are as professionals, and we're all better for it.

Every time we step outside our work space bubble, we get fresh perspectives and get the opportunity to discover new ideas and help shape the next generation of builders and leaders. It’s extremely energizing, and what I've found is that the ripple effects of giving back—big or small—always seem to come full circle.

🚀 In a field that evolves as fast as tech does, staying connected to the broader community keeps us grounded, inspired, and reminded of why we do what we do.

💡 What's your favorite way to get involved in your local or broader industry's community?

If you are interested in attending, please follow the link below to register. No preparation required! Just bring yourself and a positive attitude. Details are in the Evenbrite.

People who seem fearless aren’t certain.

They just learned to move while afraid.

Most people don’t get stuck from lack of talent.
They get stuck protecting comfort instead of growth.

They wait to “feel ready.”
They avoid what might expose gaps.
Chase perfection instead of progress.

But here’s the truth:
Every confident person you admire once felt unqualified too.
They just showed up anyway.

Because courage doesn’t follow confidence -
It creates it.

Here’s how to build that courage 👇

1. Get comfortable being a beginner
Every master started clueless.
Stop pretending to know. Start asking to learn.

2. Growth starts where ego ends
The bravest words are “I don’t know.”
Every time you say them, a new door opens.

3. Speak up when you feel small
Imposter syndrome hates honesty.
Say it out loud - take its power back.

4. Collect lessons, not labels
You’re not “bad” at something - just early at it.
Every skill looks messy in chapter one.

5. Stay curious under pressure
Ego says “I should know this.”
Curiosity asks “What can I learn here?”

6. Surround yourself with stretchers
Be around people who make you rise, not retreat.
Comfortable rooms rarely create growth.

7. Shed what no longer fits
Each new level asks you to release- 
an old belief, habit,
or identity that no longer belongs.

You won’t always see the path.
Sometimes it only appears after you take the step.

That’s the paradox of growth:
Clarity follows motion -
Not the other way around.

So keep moving.
Progress is built, not found -
And courage is what makes it real.

♻ Repost to help someone move even while afraid.
➕ Follow Mike Leber for daily insights on leadership and growth.

Welcome to the BSides South Jersey group, the scrappy underdog of the Northeast cybersecurity scene. While our neighboring cities in the North and the West host the big, bustling events, we're building something different here in the shadows of the Pine Barrens, home of the legendary Jersey Devil.

Just like the mythical beast that stalks our woods, we represent the raw, unpolished, and sometimes chaotic underbelly of infosec. We're a community for those who prefer hands-on hacking over corporate keynotes, for the researchers and students who operate on the fringes, and for the security enthusiasts who are ready to get their hands dirty.

This isn't about shiny presentations or polished sales pitches. It's about authentic conversations, deep-dives into vulnerabilities, and the kind of networking that happens organically. We're the BSides event that lives up to the original spirit of the movement: community-driven, accessible, and full of surprising discoveries.

Join us to find your tribe, share your insights, and connect with the real heroes of cybersecurity. After all, sometimes the most interesting things are found where the big-city lights don't reach.

Excited to share that the pre-proof of our paper "Detecting and Explaining Structural Changes in an Evolving Graph using a Martingale" is now available in Pattern Recognition (Elsevier):


In this work we:
- Represent an evolving network as a sequence of graph snapshots and extract multiple centrality and spectral feature streams.
- Construct an exchangeability-test martingale for each feature using non-conformity scores and a principled betting function.
- Aggregate those into an additive martingale, whose threshold crossing via Ville's inequality provides a rigorous false-alarm-controlled change-point detector.
- Show that the exact Shapley value for each feature at detection time equals its martingale contribution—enabling precise, "closed-form" attribution without costly approximations like SHAP.

We validate on synthetic graph models (Erdős-Rényi, Barabási-Albert, Newman-Watts-Strogatz) and on the MIT Reality social-proximity dataset, demonstrating tight F₁/delay trade-offs and interpretable feature attributions for real-world network shifts.

Vibe coding in the enterprise? Proceed with caution—and clarity.

AI tools that turn plain English into working code are having a moment. “Vibe coding” promises speed, accessibility, and democratized development. But let’s not confuse code generation with software engineering.

Because code that works isn’t the same as code that lasts. Vibe-coded systems often:

1. Lack architecture
2. Ignore security and compliance
3. Hide logic in conversations, not documentation
4. Break under scale - with no blueprint to fix them

We have seen this story before. From Excel macros to shadow IT. Fast becomes fragile. Useful becomes unmaintainable. What starts as innovation becomes a liability.

But here’s the reframe: vibe coding does have a role. Just not as your production pipeline.

Use it for what it’s best at: rapid prototyping, interface sketching, and idea exploration. In a sandbox. With clear boundaries. And a handoff to real engineers when it’s time to scale.

Experiment freely. Deploy responsibly.

The key takeaway: Vibe coding is a sketchpad, not a scaffold. Use it to accelerate discovery - not to shortcut discipline.

Would love to learn from your experiences with vibe coding?


 #VibeCoding #AIinTech #ShadowIT #SoftwareEngineering #TechStrategy #LLM #EnterpriseAI #RapidPrototyping #InnovationVsSustainability #ResponsibleAI

For the love of everything that is holy in the world, please PLEASE take time to personalize your GPT model so it can sound like you, and my feed can look like actual people wrote it— and make me want to read your content!

It’s not you, it’s your GPT!

To make content yours, avoid/delete/rewrite (if at all possible):

• Contrast framing (the classic “not X, but Y” such as “This isn’t a feature, it’s a movement”
• Purpose compression (short sentences that reduce complexity into intent, such as “The signal is clear”)
• Minimalism that feels engineered (Statements such as “designed to listen”, or “trust, operationalized”)

Above all, if you were never a writer before and are now posting best-selling, philosophical content that is not aligned with your brand, please stop!

I really do want to engage with your content. As long as it sounds like it’s YOURS!

Brand it! Please brand it. It takes one minute!

Miri

#AIWorldProblems #Stop

Most people told me I was too late to the game.
I left corporate at 47.
Got married at 45.

We're taught early that potential has a shelf life.

If you don't hit certain milestones by a certain age, 
you missed your chance.

I don’t believe that anymore.

I know too many people who built something real after 40:
→ After a layoff
→ After divorce
→ After illness
→ After years of playing it safe

My friend beat cancer at 42. Then went to medical school.
She's 67 now. Still practicing. One of the best in women's health.

Bernie Marcus got fired at 46.
He could've seen it as the end.

Instead, he started Home Depot at 49.
Today it's worth $343 billion.

Here's what I've learned:

The first 40 years weren't wasted. They built me.

The grief, the mistakes, the repairs, the hard lessons I didn't want to learn.

All of it matters now at 56.

There's no deadline on becoming who you're meant to be.

If you're rebuilding, restarting, or finally doing the thing you've been putting off, you're not behind.

You're right on time.

📌 Save this for the days doubt gets loud.
♻️ Share it with someone who thinks they're behind.
🔔 Follow Melina Panetta for more on building after 40
___
