I’m deeply honored to be featured by CIO TIMES as one of 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟱 𝗠𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗜𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗶𝗰 𝗕𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗧𝗼 𝗪𝗮𝘁𝗰𝗵 𝗶𝗻 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟱. I dedicate this recognition to every woman entrepreneur daring to make a difference in the world!

When Stacey and I co-founded Empressa AI, we took a leap of faith to solve a problem we couldn’t ignore. 

Today, our innovation speaks for itself. We built 𝗮 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁-𝗼𝗳-𝗶𝘁𝘀-𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗔𝗜 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺 𝗯𝘆 𝘄𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗻, 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘄𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗻, 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗰𝗹𝗼𝘀𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗽𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗴𝗮𝗽.

Empressa wears two crowns: 

👑  We’re enabling women experts to build their digital legacy and earn royalties every time their insights guide others. This means that a mid-career strategist in Ohio or a grassroots founder in Nairobi can transform her hard-earned experience into a source of income and impact. 
👑 Simultaneously, rising women leaders gain 24/7 access to tailored mentorship, bridging the gap between potential and opportunity.

At the heart of this journey are a few core beliefs that guide me every day:

	•	𝗘𝗺𝗽𝗮𝘁𝗵𝘆 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗹𝘆𝘀𝘁: “At Empressa, we don’t just build networks—we build trust, empathy, and lifelong connections.”  Real innovation starts with listening and understanding.
	•	𝗜𝗻𝗻𝗼𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗹𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻: We can’t wait 134 years for gender parity in the workforce – I believe #AI can be an equalizer to accelerate equity. When women build the tech, they build a future for everyone.
	•	𝗖𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽: True leadership requires the courage to show up authentically, even when it’s hard. I’ve learned that owning my whole story — the failures, lessons, and triumphs — is actually one of my greatest strengths.

I’m extremely grateful and proud of the team behind Empressa taking this mission to life (Ronnie Hamilton, Siddhartha C., Akshat Rana,PMP®, Laura Jimenez and Viktoriya Lindner), our Founding Members (tagged below) who are the lifeblood of our community, and the continued support from our Empressalies!

If you’re a woman expert or a woman rising leader or a supporter of women, join us at Empressa.ai!

🔐 Security is everyone’s responsibility. But how do we make it practical before the commit even happens?

We all say “shift security left”, yet many teams still rely on vague “check security” prompts. That is not structured, and it is definitely not intentional.

After attending today’s “Agentic AI for DevSecOps” session by Calin Lupas and Emmanuel Knafo at Microsoft #TechConnect, I have a solid goodie to share
👉 

This repo contains a set of custom GitHub Copilot agents, each tailored to specific security related tasks. A very practical way to introduce pre-commit security thinking into everyday developer workflows.

How to use them effectively:
- Good idea: copy these agents into the .github/agents folder of your repo
- Very good idea: place them in a shared .private-github repo at the org  level so they are available to everyone, both in IDEs and with the Coding agent
- Homework: convert these agents into agent skills so they can be reused across any agent you work with

This is how “security is everyone’s responsibility” becomes something developers can actually act on, consistently and early.

Awesome Copilot now includes Agentic Workflows!

A community-driven space to share real, end‑to‑end agentic workflows that plan, implement, iterate, and automate real work.

You can now run GitHub Agentic Workflows in your SDLC with reproducible context dependencies using microsoft/apm-action GitHub Action.

GitHub Agentic Workflows lets AI agents run inside your CI/CD pipeline. Triggered on a PR. On a schedule. On a label change. At enterprise scale. The agent reads your code, makes decisions, and acts — over thousands of repos if you like.

But here's what happens at scale: the agent's behavior depends on prompts, instructions, and skills scattered across repos. Someone updates an instruction upstream. Your agent silently changes behavior. No diff. No review. No trace.

That's the gap. Your app's code is versioned and pinned. The agent's knowledge is not.

microsoft/apm-action closes that gap. It treats prompts and instructions as dependencies — versioned, locked, reproducible. The same agent with the same context, every run. Whether that's Tuesday at 2pm or Saturday at 3am.

We've enforced this discipline on code for decades. It was only a matter of time before agents needed it too.

Agents in CI/CD is a new Agentic SDLC frontier. Reproducibility and security is what will make it production-ready at scale.

microsoft/apm-action wraps Agent Package Manager (APM), both shipping as OSS, MIT-licensed community projects to help you manage agent configuration and context as dependency packages.

One in five code reviews on GitHub now runs through Copilot.

60 million reviews since April. 12,000+ organizations have it on by default. What changed: Copilot code review moved to an agentic model that retrieves repo context, reads linked issues and PRs, and plans its review strategy before commenting. It remembers patterns across reviews. And in 29% of reviews, it says nothing at all -- no comments, no noise, just silence when there's nothing worth flagging.

One of the most common complaints about automated code review is noise. Comments nobody reads. Suggestions that miss the point of the change. GitHub's response: treat silence as a feature. If the agent can't find something consequential, it keeps quiet. When it does comment, it averages about 5.1 comments per review and attaches feedback to logical code ranges instead of single lines.

WEX is running it across every repository and shipping ~30% more code.
Their most active contributors are the heaviest Copilot users.

Are you measuring GitHub Copilot usage… or real impact? 🤔

Many teams proudly track seats, active users, and suggestion acceptance in GitHub Copilot.

That’s adoption.

But leadership conversations are shifting toward something else:
👉 Is AI actually improving engineering performance?

This is where gh-devlake changes the game.

Built as a GitHub CLI extension, gh-devlake connects Copilot usage data with real delivery metrics across your engineering systems. More details and how to start: 

Instead of looking at AI stats in isolation, you can correlate:

- Copilot usage
- PR cycle time
- Deployment frequency
- Change failure rate
- Mean time to recovery

Now we’re talking about impact.

What I like about this approach is that it moves the conversation from opinion to evidence. You’re no longer asking whether AI “feels” productive. You’re analyzing how AI adoption aligns with actual delivery outcomes in your organization.

For engineering leaders, this is critical.
AI investments need defensible ROI narratives. gh-devlake gives you the foundation to build them using your own DevOps data.

We are entering the phase where AI in engineering is not about experimentation anymore. It is about measurable value.

Are you already correlating Copilot adoption with delivery metrics? Or still tracking usage alone?

GitHub Copilot CLI just hit GA! 🎉 

Not to over-hype, but I'm actually pretty excited about this. I've been using it since preview and it's become my go-to for anything that starts in a terminal. But the part that actually changed how I work isn't the coding.

I use Copilot CLI with WorkIQ and my work notes to close the loop between different silos. Meeting notes in one place, tasks in another, context scattered across emails and docs. Copilot CLI ties them together and helps me organize my thinking, not just my code. WorkIQ lets Copilot access meeting recaps and relevant documents to augment my notes.

Copilot CLI also has the concept of "infinite sessions." Sessions persist locally as structured state files, with compaction checkpoints and persistent artifacts. I've been running the same session for my notes for over a month with no degradation. Repository memory means Copilot learns your conventions and preferences across sessions, so each new session starts smarter than the last. It's not just "long conversations" but durable working memory.

Background delegation is the other one worth knowing about. Or tell Copilot CLI to kick of parallel tasks (like code reviews with Opus and Codex) and it'll run them in the background, updating you when they finish.

You can get really meta and open Copilot CLI in the terminal in VS Code. Or if you've been waiting for Copilot in vim or Emacs, here you go!

Le 11 mars, au Microsoft AI Tour à Paris Expo, Porte de Versailles, plusieurs d’entre elles viendront partager leur expérience : Amundi, Ardian, AXA, Badabook, Guerbet, MAD Paris, Pernod Ricard, Saint-Gobain, Schneider Electric, Skribi - Le compagnon d'écriture, Sonepar, Thales, TotalEnergies.

GitHub just introduced Agentic Workflows. Yes, you can now wire generative AI directly into your CI/CD.

Let me be very clear: There are parts of the SDLC where AI has no business making autonomous decisions. Generative AI is probabilistic. Your production pipeline should not be.

Use it for pattern recognition, cross checking, refactoring suggestions:
– Checking whether a PR actually implements the acceptance criteria
– Verifying that documentation and tests reflect the feature
– Opening cleanup PRs for code quality and structural improvements

Do not use it if the potential blast radius includes production, money, or regulatory exposure:
– Deployments
– Deciding whether a security, compliance, or production bug “really matters”
– Provisioning or modifying cloud infrastructure
– Anything that must be deterministic, auditable, and legally defensible

If you follow me, you know I am not anti AI. What I am is very serious about understanding where non deterministic systems belong.

CI/CD is the control plane of your business. Treat it like one.

𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗖𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗹𝗼𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝘂𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗜𝗗𝗘.
I’m currently building POLY𝖢𝖫𝖠𝖶 — a personal project where I'm using the 𝗚𝗶𝘁𝗛𝘂𝗯 𝗖𝗼𝗽𝗶𝗹𝗼𝘁 𝗦𝗗𝗞 to move AI assistance out of the code editor and into a fully autonomous agentic experience.
By leveraging the 𝗦𝗗𝗞 alongside 𝖠𝗓𝗎𝗋𝖾 𝖡𝗈𝗍 𝖲𝖾𝗋𝗏𝗂𝖼𝖾 and 𝖠𝗓𝗎𝗋𝖾 𝖢𝗈𝗆𝗆𝗎𝗇𝗂𝖼𝖺𝗍𝗂𝗈𝗇 𝖲𝖾𝗋𝗏𝗂𝖼𝖾𝗌, I’ve enabled Copilot to live in your messaging apps or even reach out via real-time phone calls. It’s no longer just a chatbot; it’s a self-extending agent with a native scheduling system for cron jobs.

𝗞𝗲𝘆 𝗖𝗮𝗽𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀:
🔹 𝗦𝗲𝗹𝗳-𝗘𝘅𝘁𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴: The agent actually 𝗐𝗋𝗂𝗍𝖾𝗌 𝗂𝗍𝗌 𝗈𝗐𝗇 𝗌𝗄𝗂𝗅𝗅𝗌 at runtime. If it lacks a tool, it builds it. 
🔹 𝗣𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀𝗽𝗮𝗰𝗲 & 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆: It maintains its own environment across sessions—allowing it to manage things like 𝗅𝗈𝖼𝖺𝗅 𝖲𝖰𝖫𝗂𝗍𝖾 𝖽𝖺𝗍𝖺𝖻𝖺𝗌𝖾𝗌 or complex file structures autonomously. 
🔹 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗦𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗱𝘂𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴: It manages its own tasks — using its 𝗈𝗐𝗇 𝖻𝗋𝗈𝗐𝗌𝖾𝗋 or maintaining repos — and reports back proactively via 𝖠𝗓𝗎𝗋𝖾 𝖡𝗈𝗍 𝖲𝖾𝗋𝗏𝗂𝖼𝖾. 
🔹 𝗔𝗻𝘆𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗔𝗰𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀: Use the TUI, Web UI, or have it 𝗉𝗁𝗈𝗇𝖾 𝗒𝗈𝗎 via 𝖠𝗓𝗎𝗋𝖾 𝖢𝗈𝗆𝗆𝗎𝗇𝗂𝖼𝖺𝗍𝗂𝗈𝗇 𝖲𝖾𝗋𝗏𝗂𝖼𝖾𝗌 when a critical deployment needs your attention.

⚠️ 𝖠𝗇 𝖤𝗑𝗉𝖾𝗋𝗂𝗆𝖾𝗇𝗍𝖺𝗅 𝖡𝗎𝗂𝗅𝖽: This is an independent, community-built tool. It is currently in an 𝖾𝖺𝗋𝗅𝗒 𝖾𝗑𝗉𝖾𝗋𝗂𝗆𝖾𝗇𝗍𝖺𝗅 stage. Polyclaw operates with a high degree of autonomy under 𝗬𝗢𝗨𝗥 identity. It carries your permissions and tokens; it’s built for developers who want to push the limits of agentic workflows while staying mindful of the risks.

If you’ve seen the recent traction around 𝖮𝗉𝖾𝗇𝖢𝗅𝖺𝗐, this is my take on a native evolution for those deep in the GitHub ecosystem.

I’m still deep in the development phase, so stay tuned — 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗼𝗻.

🧭Cap sur l'Agentic DevOps avec GitHub Copilot !

Vendredi dernier, avec mes collègues de AXA en France , nous nous sommes plongés au cœur de l’écosystème GitHub Copilot  dans les locaux de Microsoft.

Un immense merci à Daniel Meppiel  et Thierry Madkaud  pour la qualité de leurs présentations. Leur expertise nous a permis de décrypter des concepts qui transforment déjà notre manière de concevoir le logiciel.

💡Les piliers technologiques abordés :
Le framework PROSE : Nous avons mis en pratique cette approche structurée qui permet d'industrialiser nos interactions avec l'IA. Finis les prompts aléatoires, place à une ingénierie rigoureuse pour des résultats plus fiables. 

Agentic Workflow & Continuous AI : L'IA ne se contente plus de suggérer du code, elle devient un partenaire autonome capable d'orchestrer des tâches complexes au sein du workflow de développement.  

APM (Agent Package Manager) & Copilot CLI : Focus sur la gestion propre des briques d'IA et découverte de la Ralph Wiggum loop pour une expérience en ligne de commande boostée. 

L'évolution vers l'Agentic DevOps : Une réflexion passionnante sur la mutation de nos pratiques. L'intégration d'agents intelligents redéfinit totalement le cycle de vie applicatif.

🛠️ Mise en pratique

Votre stratégie de documentation doit évoluer pour l'ère de l'IA.

Le constat : les assistants de code IA comme GitHub Copilot indexent vos repositories. Si votre documentation est dans Confluence ou Notion, elle est invisible pour les outils que vos développeurs utilisent au quotidien.

Les chiffres parlent d'eux-mêmes : 
→ Recherche DORA : les équipes avec une documentation de qualité ont 2,4x plus de chances d'atteindre de meilleures performances de delivery 
→ Google a constaté que 48% des ingénieurs citaient la mauvaise documentation comme leur frein n°1; jusqu'à ce qu'ils placent les docs à côté du code 
→ Le markdown structuré améliore la précision de récupération IA de jusqu'à 35% vs du contenu non structuré

Le pattern qui émerge : documentation as code, directement dans vos repos en markdown. 
Pas seulement le README.md, mais les décisions d'architecture, les guides d'onboarding, les réfèrences API; là où votre assistant IA peut les trouver.

Il ne s'agit pas d'abandonner les wikis. Il s'agit de reconnaître que la proximité du contexte compte plus que jamais quand l'IA est votre pair programmer.

Quelle est l'approche de votre équipe ? Toujours centralisée dans des outils externes, ou docs plus proches du code ? Une approche hybride avec les serveurs MCP ?

Today we’re launching the GitHub Copilot SDK. 

Coding is the first place AI agents have real product-market fit, with daily, sustained use by millions of developers, which means the hardest problems have already been exercised at scale.  

With the Copilot SDK, we’re making that runtime available to every developer, allowing you to embed the same agent execution engine that powers Copilot into any app with just a few lines of code. Planning, execution, tool calling, and controls are designed as a single system, shaped by real workloads and real constraints. We’ve been building toward this across our platforms for some time, and this is the moment those lessons become something the world can build on directly. 

The SDK is available today in technical preview with a GitHub Copilot subscription.

🔗 Learn more about the SDK on the GitHub blog ( then visit the SDK repo ( to start building.

✨ Offsite OCP SaaS : une journée qui nous ressemble ✨

J’ai eu le plaisir d’organiser notre dernier offsite OCP SaaS BNP Paribas, une journée qui me tenait particulièrement à cœur.

Cet événement a été l’occasion d’accueillir 3 nouvelles offres au sein du domaine, ainsi que de nouvelles personnes dans l’équipe, et de les intégrer pleinement autour d’un moment fort de cohésion.

Dès le départ, le mot d’ordre était clair :
👉 cohésion,
👉 partage de notre vision stratégique interne,
👉 et fun.

C’est avec un immense plaisir que nous avons réalisé cet offsite dans les locaux de notre partenaire de longue date Microsoft , que je remercie chaleureusement pour son accueil et son engagement à nos côtés. Mentions spéciales à Loïc Cadiot, Bruno Invernizzi , Oleg M., Tarik Labidi, Tugdual Grall et Thierry Madkaud  pour la gestion de l’organisation et la qualité des présentations. 

La matinée a été rythmée par deux thématiques clés :
🔹 Dynamics 365
🔹 les innovations autour de l’agentique, notamment avec GitHub

Deux présentations et démonstrations animées par les équipes Microsoft, qui ont suscité beaucoup de questions, d’échanges et beaucoup d’intérêt.

L’après-midi, j’ai souhaité aller plus loin avec une approche très concrète : avec l’aide des équipes Microsoft, j’ai imaginé et animé un hackathon — ou plutôt un “agentathon” 🤖
L’objectif : permettre à chaque équipe de développer un jeu vidéo en vibe coding, afin d’illustrer la puissance des agents IA pour construire quelque chose de tangible en seulement quelques heures.

👉 Les résultats ont été bluffants
👉 Des équipes très engagées
👉 Une énergie collective incroyable

Une journée riche en découvertes, en innovation, et surtout axée sur l’humain — des valeurs qui reflètent parfaitement l’ADN de notre domaine OCP SaaS.

Un immense merci à toutes les équipes impliquées.
Hâte de construire la suite ensemble 🚀

Merci Damien Lassalle pour ta confiance sur l’organisation de cette belle journée!

AI maturity is forcing a structural rethink of engineering organizations. Agile created speed. Agentile creates intelligence. The shift is already underway.

This moment requires a reconsideration of how engineering organizations operate. In my work as Chief Dev Advisor, the conversations keep converging: #Agentic #DevOps, AI‑assisted coding, and agentic experiences are redefining how teams collaborate, innovate, and deliver value. AI adoption is uneven across the industry, but the direction is uniform. Teams built for this new reality –Agentile teams– combine lean, #agile, and agentic principles to enable humans and AI to work side by side at higher velocity and clarity.

𝗖𝗵𝗮𝘁𝗚𝗣𝗧 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝗱𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝟮 𝘀𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗱𝘀. 𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗔𝗖𝗧𝗨𝗔𝗟𝗟𝗬 𝗵𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘀 𝗯𝗲𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗰𝗲𝗻𝗲𝘀? 🤖⚡

Most people think it's magic ✨ Engineers know it's a symphony of distributed systems, GPU clusters, and high-dimensional mathematics 🧮

I just published "𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗟𝗶𝗳𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗮𝗻 𝗔𝗜 𝗤𝘂𝗲𝗿𝘆" — a deep dive that traces the millisecond-by-millisecond journey of a single prompt through ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗮𝘀𝗸: "Write a haiku about a robot loving a cat" 🐱

🔹 Your text is shattered into 𝘁𝗼𝗸𝗲𝗻𝘀 (sub-word units) 
🔹 Each token becomes a 𝘃𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗿 of thousands of floating-point numbers 
🔹 The Transformer's 𝗠𝘂𝗹𝘁𝗶-𝗛𝗲𝗮𝗱 𝗔𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 calculates relationships 
🔹 The 𝗠𝗟𝗣 Multi-Layer Perceptron retrieves knowledge (robots = metal 🦾, cats = fur 🐈, haikus = 5-7-5) 
🔹 The model predicts 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝘁𝗼𝗸𝗲𝗻 𝗮𝘁 𝗮 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲 using probability distributions 📊 
🔹 The 𝗞𝗩 𝗖𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗲 prevents redundant calculations 🚀

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝘀𝘂𝗹𝘁: "Metal heart beats fast / Soft fur purrs against the steel / Love knows no code base" 💙

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗸𝘀 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗺𝗮𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗶𝘀 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆:
⚙️ JSON payloads hitting edge nodes 
⚙️ TLS-encrypted routing to GPU clusters
⚙️ Gigabytes of model weights in HBM 
⚙️ 96 stacked Transformer layers 
⚙️ Positional encodings preserving word order 
⚙️ Autoregressive loops generating text token-by-token

This isn't just a curiosity question. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗮 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺 𝗱𝗲𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻. 💡

AI is causing a new dev pattern: I heard this yesterday: I show my stakeholders a demo, we generate 10-12 exciting ideas, and by end of next day, 80% are already in production. Then I let them know: 'It's live — what do you think?"

People who fret about AI replacing dev jobs are missing the point. When you can create value 10x or even 100x more quickly, you get more headcount, not less.

Another example: I paired with someone two days ago who made a circa-2005 Google Analytics clone in less than 30 minutes. (One JavaScript snippet, sending events to Google Cloud Run, PubSub, and BigQuery.). It will eventually ingest 20MM events/month, way above the 1MM Google Analytics unsampled limit.  (We wrote the reporting engine in Python Streamlit today.)

The longest part of the process? Getting permission to create PubSub subscribers. Not coding.

These types of obstacles are what Steve Yegge and I called "barbed wire" in our Vibe Coding book. But I never saw as vividly as how critical it is to have someone from infosec or admin rights on the team — all your wildest dreams are blocked by waiting to get access rights.

Another heartbreaking form of barbed wire? People stuck in protracted sprint planning processes, endless backlog grooming meetings about what features should work on next and which need to be pushed into next quarter... 

The new bottlenecks are organizational, not technical — let's go fix them!

Exciting news for Enterprise developers from Ignite!

Developers shouldn't have to juggle manual installs, worry about unverified extensions, or chase compliance exceptions just to get their favorite tools. Today, we're excited to announce Private Marketplace for VS Code—a dedicated, enterprise-ready hub that puts you in full control of how extensions are sourced, reviewed, and distributed to your dev teams.

What if your AI assistant could follow your rules, reflect your stack, and automate your engineering workflows with precision?

💡GitHub just introduced custom agents in Copilot, enabling teams to extend AI into observability, IaC, security, databases, deployments, incident response and more. These agents are defined in plain Markdown and available across Copilot CLI, VS Code, and github.com. 

Each custom agent is built from a clear set of components that shape how it behaves inside your environment:
• A detailed definition that outlines the agent’s purpose, capabilities, and behavior
• A set of MCP tools the agent can work with, providing structured and secure access to systems and APIs
• Metainformation for handoffs, allowing agents to guide Copilot into the next logical workflow step and support multi-step tasks

This structure moves AI-native development from concept to practice. Teams can encode knowledge, maintain standards, and orchestrate workflows through reusable agent specifications tailored to their engineering context.

Learn more and start using custom agents today:

APM (Agent Package Manager) has a new home in the Microsoft Open Source GitHub organization.

I created APM to solve a problem I kept seeing: AI coding agents are powerful, but configuring them consistently across a team is still manual and fragile. 

Some teams are already using it in production. Moving APM to the Microsoft Open Source organization gives it better visibility, proper OSS governance, and a place where the community can keep building it together.

To be clear: APM is a community-driven open source project, not a supported Microsoft product. If you have questions, bugs, or ideas — open an issue or a discussion on the repo.

If you would have told me 3 years ago that I’d be graduating from business school with an MBA, I would never have believed you. Coming from the social impact and international development sector, going to business school isn’t a very typical path, but I’m so encouraged to see that changing through the diversity of my MBA cohort at Oxford and other business schools – I gained so many network-building opportunities and transferable skills, from leadership development to venture-building and systems analysis, that I hope to take back into the sector.🌍

Last year was a whirlwind! I’m so thrilled to have completed my MBA at Saïd Business School, University of Oxford with a distinction and Dean’s List Honours. Milestones like these are not realised with the flick of a switch. It takes so much hard work, curiosity, dedication and a village of support. I’m very grateful to my village of friends, family, mentors and classmates for encouraging me to follow this through.🙌 
 
This year I’m looking forward to the next chapter 📖 where I’ll be joining the team at the LSE’s Marshall Institute & 100x Impact Accelerator to support their mission to scale and build a supportive ecosystem for social ventures and venture philanthropy!

C'est en dépassant les frontières qu'on à réussi à répondre à la problématique de la pénurie des talents en France tout en optimisant vos coûts opérationnels.

Converge is #DecarbonisingConstruction! 🏗️💚🌍 Hot off the tails of #COP26, watch Raphael Scheps, our CEO, explain how Converge is decarbonising construction.

Scalable, digital solutions are going to be key to hit our global decarbonisation targets by 2030 and 2050, and we’re more excited than ever about the impact we can have to support these goals.

A big thank you to Innovate UK and the UK Research and Innovation alongside our consortium partners (including Laing O'Rourke, Keltbray, Mace, Royal BAM Group nv, and many others) on the transforming construction challenge and helping to make it all happen!

and thank you to AKT II for letting us use part of their great RNZC grant video. 

#sustainability #UKNetZero #CarbonZero #Sustainability #decarbonization #BuildTheFuture #constructiontech #ai

I don't post much here — mostly when I'm hiring. So if you're seeing this, hi. 👋

I'm looking for a Senior Software Engineer to join my team at Apple, and I want to give you an honest picture of what the role actually is — because the title will mislead you.

It says "Quality." It is not a QA job.

What it actually is: you'd be building the platform infrastructure that Retail & Marcom Engineering relies on to ship confidently at scale — across 100+ languages, millions of customers, and some of Apple's highest-visibility launches.

That means shared CI/CD services, LLM-powered developer tooling (RAG copilots, agentic test generation, natural-language CI gates), federated APIs, and the evaluation harnesses that make it all trustworthy. Real platform engineering. Real AI work, not just the word.

The kind of engineer I'm hoping to find has shipped something LLM-powered in a production context, has opinions about CI flakiness and runner infrastructure, and wants to shape platform direction — not just close tickets.

Node or Python (ideally both), 5+ years of experience, and at least one shipped AI feature are the core bar. Seniority preference is 8+ years with platform or automation depth.

If this sounds like you, apply here: 

And if you know someone who'd be a great fit, I'd appreciate a share.

If you have experience building operating systems, compilers, synthesis tools, or working on advanced error‑correction techniques, this is the kind of problem space you’ve been waiting for.

We’re a fast‑moving, highly collaborative, multi‑disciplinary team of quantum researchers and engineers, and we value people with a strong bias for action - engineers who want to shape the next computing platform from first principles.

Attention early-in-career tech folks! A number of jobs open through the Microsoft AI Development Acceleration Program. Software eng and applied science in Redmond WA and Cambridge MA.

🚀 Exciting Opportunity for Recent Grads: Associate Solutions Architect at AWS 🎓 

Are you passionate about AI and cloud technology? Ready to launch your career at the forefront of innovation? Amazon Web Services is seeking talented early-career technologists for an Associate Solutions Architect position starting May 11, 2026.

Locations: Arlington, VA | Austin, TX | Seattle, WA

Why This Role?
This is your opportunity to work alongside experienced AWS architects and learn how to design real-world cloud and AI architectures used by some of the most innovative companies in the world. You'll work directly with customers ranging from startups to global enterprises, helping them architect scalable, resilient solutions that solve real business problems. You'll be a trusted customer advocate, guiding organizations through cloud and AI adoption and best practices.

AI at the Core
We're especially excited about candidates with experience in AI, Large Language Models (LLMs), and Machine Learning. As AI continues to reshape every industry, you'll be at the center of helping organizations harness these transformative technologies on AWS.

What You'll Do:
✅  Design, architect, and prototype solutions that address complex customer challenges
✅  Evangelize AWS services and best practices across diverse organizations
✅  Partner with developers, architects, and business stakeholders to accelerate cloud and AI adoption
✅  Translate customer feedback into actionable requirements for product teams

What We're Looking For:
• Bachelor's degree in Computer Science, Computer Engineering, or related field (graduation date May 2024 - April 2026)
• Programming experience (Java, Python, Ruby, Node.js, Go, Rust, C#)
• Experience building applications with AI, including LLMs or generative AI models
• Excellent communication skills for technical and non-technical audiences, including public speaking
• Background in analytics, security, storage, DevOps, or application development

Bonus Points:
• AWS or industry certifications
• Experience with frameworks, design patterns, and software delivery

Start Date: May 11, 2026

This is your chance to build the future developing both your technical expertise and business acumen. If you're a self-starter ready to make a significant impact and help customers craft highly scalable, flexible cloud architectures, we want to hear from you!

#AWS #CloudComputing #SolutionsArchitect #AI #GenerativeAI #MachineLearning #CareerOpportunity #TechJobs #CloudArchitecture #Innovation #ArlingtonVA #AustinTX #SeattleWA

Interested? Apply now and come build the future with us! 🌟

How to use AI for your next job interview

Noam Segal, my community research lead, interviewed 30 current and recent job seekers about how they use AI throughout the interview process.

What he found went far beyond polishing resumes. People had built entire systems tailor-made for their own situations: ways to get feedback on what they actually said in interviews, methods to predict questions before walking in, workflows to surface stories they didn’t know they had. Each person he spoke with had figured out how to use AI for one or two pieces of the interviewing puzzle.

As he was pulling together a research report from these conversations, he quickly realized that most people on the job market are stressed and anxious enough. The best value he could offer wasn’t a list of tips but, instead, a way to plug-and-play the hard work these participants have already done. 

So he changed direction, took every interview AI technique that worked for these participants, added a layer of professional coaching techniques, and built a free Claude Code–based coach you can plug-and-play into your interview process today.

The coach helps you with every step of the process:
1. Scores your actual interview responses (not what you think you said)
2. Mines your experience for stories you didn't know you had
3. Tracks the gap between how you think you did and how you actually did
4. Runs mock interviews that push back and interrupt you
5. Coaches post-offer salary negotiation with exact scripts and fallback language
...and much more

Grab it here (totally free):

To the engineers, product managers, and customer-focused builders in logistics tech: we're hiring.

There's a narrative taking hold in the tech sector right now that the era of the human builder is shrinking. 

That as AI advances, the need for brilliant engineers, product managers, and support teams simply goes away.

At Clear.ai, we take a fundamentally different view.

Building true AI-first software doesn't reduce the need for great engineering and deep product empathy — it elevates it. It enables humans to focus on what only they can do best: solving complex, high-leverage problems, thinking creatively, and shipping incredible user experiences.

There's been a lot of movement in the logistics tech market this week. But we believe the future of logistics needs more builders, not fewer. 

If that's the kind of team you want to be part of, let's talk.

Exa (raised $85M at $700M valuation) is hiring in San Francisco - and they sponsor visas. 

They're growing fast and need engineers, designers, support, and recruiters across all levels.

2021 - Founded in SF to build search infrastructure for AI
2025 - $700M valuation with 50+ team members

Will Bryk and Jeffrey Wang recognized early that building real search infra for AI requires global talent - so they actively sponsor visas for exceptional candidates.

Here's what they're looking for:

1/  Head of AI Research ($250K-$500K + equity) - Lead LLM and information retrieval research. Requires 4+ years AI research experience.

2/  Infrastructure Engineer ($150K-$300K + equity) - Build GPU cluster orchestration on $20M systems and scale AWS batch jobs across tens of thousands of machines.

3/  Automation Engineer ($150K-$300K + equity) - Optimize internal processes with AI. Heavy use of Cursor, Claude, Devin.

4/  Research, Evals ($150K-$300K + equity) - Design frameworks that define what perfect search looks like. Build pipelines that track quality across billions of documents.

5/  Designer ($150K-$280K + equity) - Own brand identity and developer experience design. Strong visual design + product judgment.

6/  Founding Security Engineer ($150K-$300K + equity) - Build security infrastructure for web-scale data processing and own compliance and system hardening efforts.

7/  Head of Data Partnerships ($180K-$300K + above market equity) - Run the full partnership cycle - outreach, negotiation, close. Make the data acquisition decisions that shape Exa’s competitive edge.

8/  Support Engineer ($120K-$200K + equity) - Handle technical support and automate systems and work with Jeeyoung Kim (Head of Engineering) to scale support.

9/  GTM Recruiter ($100K-$180K + equity) - Build recruiting infrastructure using in-house AI tools and work with Stanislava Tarasiuk to scale the team.

This list isn’t exhaustive. More roles on Exa’s careers page - with visa sponsorship for international candidates (STEM OPT, H-1B, O-1, E-3, OPT).

interactions.original_post:
Yesterday I had the honor and pleasure of being a panelist in a University of Washington (UW) event promoted by SOMOS.tech 👩🏽‍💻 (We are tech) and Information School at UW.
We discussed about professional journey, and shared our interview insights and tips with the students who attended the event. 
 
I was very impressed and happy to see the engagement and enthusiasm of the students. They asked thoughtful and relevant questions and showed a genuine interest in learning more about how to pursue a career in this exciting and fast-growing area. 

I was also delighted to connect with my fellow panelists, Audrey Long, specialist in security @ Microsoft, and Victoria Wang, Product Manager @ Microsoft.  who are amazing professionals and role models in their respective areas of expertise.

I enjoyed sharing some interview tips that I learned in my career. These tips can help anyone who is looking for their ideal job. Here are some of them:

- Be yourself and honest during interviews. Focus on your strengths and eagerness to learn and grow.

- Do more than classes and college assignments to stand out. Show your passion and curiosity, collaborate with your colleagues in your own and their projects.
 
- Don't be scared of joining hackathons if you don't know the techs involved. Join them to learn and connect with people. You don't need to be an expert, you just need to be open-minded and willing to learn.

- Be proud and confident of your identity and culture, whether it's LATINX or another. Diversity is a strength, not a weakness. Share your stories and experiences that make you who you are and what you value.

- Experiment with and create your own projects using AI technologies. This will help you learn faster and keep up to date. There are many tools and platforms that you can use to try AI. Your College probably have some Azure credits for students (as Audrey mentioned well). Solve real-world problems that matter to you, or just have fun with AI and see what you can make. 

- Ask ChatGPT or Copilot to review your resume or help you prepare for an interview.

- In an interview, show concrete examples of how you collaborate with your peers at College or at your internship. Talk about one or two examples, pick your best collaboration case. Highlight the outcomes of your collaboration, and how you helped the group learn and succeed.

- To avoid sounding arrogant, when talking about your achievements and what you did, start with what your group did together and then move to the points where you made a difference and helped the group learn and succeed. This will show your humility, initiative and teamwork skills.

Thank you Luz A Mendoza García, Maho Pacheco, Lindsay Sullivan and everyone involved in the organization of the event.

Some engineers I worked with at Microsoft never got promoted.

They weren’t political.
They weren’t toxic.
They just made one fatal mistake:

They assumed great code = great career.

But code doesn’t advocate for you in calibration meetings.

People do.

Your promo packet isn’t just about impact.

It’s about perceived impact, narrated clearly, backed by people who matter.

If you can’t speak business,
If you can’t explain your work without using acronyms,
If no one outside your team knows your name…

You’re invisible.

And invisible engineers don’t get promoted.

If you’re ready to grow beyond titles and start influencing, 
this is where it begins ↓



Stop chasing titles. 
Start attracting roles.

#PM:

Have multiple offers? 
Your team(1), role(2), and project(3) are important.
Money(4) is also a factor.


Company brand(5) is a distant fifth.
--------

Recently, I've seen folks join Google and Microsoft for lesser pay and a lower position (SDE-2 to SDE-1). 
Just because they want to associate with the brand.

What the hell is going on lol. 

Brands come and go. The brand you'll be associated with the longest is YOURSELF. Choose what's best for you.

300+ rejections to working at Google, Microsoft, Tesla and a startup in a year (as an international student).

These are the things that worked for me:

- Improved my skills and resume
- Applied early (first day of application opening)
- Got referrals
- Attended conferences
- Messaged people on LinkedIn for advice, referrals and mock interviews 
- Prepared well for interviews
- Did a lot of mocks (for PM)
- Stayed consistent. This is the most important. Keep applying, keep improving your skills.

Nope, I did not hit the lottery in a year. It took 2.5 years of consistent rejections to reiterate and get better. So don’t get demotivated seeing others, your turn will come :) You do you.

Meanwhile, check out Scholarship Track.

✨ It’s official — I’m in the book 📖✨

When I first sat down for my interview with Izabela Duiwe, I didn’t get the questions in advance — she wanted my story to be as authentic as possible. I came in unprepared, and what followed was an honest, flowing conversation that brought back so many hidden memories I hadn’t realized were still with me.

In my chapter of The Women of Microsoft, I open up about the winding path I’ve taken as both a pianist and a software engineer — two passions that may seem worlds apart, but together taught me discipline, creativity, and resilience. Along the way, I share practical strategies on balancing multiple roles, managing time effectively, and protecting energy to avoid burnout. These reflections were built over years of perseverance, growth, and self-discovery.

This book is more than a publication — it’s a movement to celebrate the voices, journeys, and resilience of women in tech. Being part of this experience, alongside leaders whose journeys deeply inspired me, has been a real privilege. 

Huge thanks to Miri Rodriguez and Izabela Duiwe for bringing these stories to life! 🚀 Launching today! Order your copy here:  and I am looking forward to hear what you think! #microsoft #microsoft50 #WomenInTech

I was recently interviewed by Xiaoxi (Cynthia) Wang  from #WeWorkingWomen女仕界 —and it turned into one of the most honest conversations I’ve had about my journey.

Honestly, I spent years trying to be less of myself—less sensitive, less “different,” less visibly struggling. I thought if I just worked harder and kept my head down, the rest would sort itself out.

It didn’t.

What actually helped was learning to stop fighting the things that make me who I am. Being neurodivergent. Being a Chinese woman in rooms where I’m often the only one. The chronic pain that never went away after my car accident. These aren’t things to overcome or hide—they’re part of how I see the world, how I approach problems, why I care about what I care about.

Cynthia asked real questions and actually listened. Thank you for being willing to spend that time exploring my inner world with me.

And 女仕界 WeWorkingWomen and Sherry Zhang —thank you for building a community where I don’t have to edit myself down to fit. It’s rare to find a space that holds both the struggles and the strength.

If you’ve ever felt like you’re too much or not enough, you’re not alone. The path doesn’t have to be linear, and you don’t need to have it all figured out to start.

Article link: 

—————————————————

说实话，我花了很多年试图让自己”少一点”——少一点敏感、少一点”不一样”、少一点让人看出我在挣扎。我以为只要更努力、低头做事，其他的自然会好起来。

并没有。

真正帮到我的，是学会不再和那些让我成为我的东西对抗。神经多样性。作为华裔女性常常是房间里唯一的那一个。车祸后一直没消失的慢性疼痛。这些不是需要克服或藏起来的东西——它们是我看世界的方式，是我解决问题的方式，是我为什么在乎我在乎的事。

和小溪 Cynthia Wang 的这次对话让很多事情变清晰了。她问的是真问题，而且真的在听。谢谢你愿意花时间陪我一起探索我的内心世界。

女仕界——谢谢你们创造了一个让我不用削减自己去适应的社群。能找到一个既承载挣扎、也看见力量的空间，真的很难得。

如果你也曾觉得自己太多或者不够，你不是一个人。路不一定是直的，也不需要什么都想清楚了才能开始。

This #InternationalWomensDay feels extra special—I’m honored to have my story featured in The Women of Microsoft: Empowering Stories from the Minds that Coded the World, authored by the incredible Miri Rodriguez and Izabela Duiwe! As both a software engineer and pianist, I’ve always believed that technology and creativity go hand in hand.

This book shares the journeys of incredible women who have shaped and led Microsoft, proving that passion, resilience, and innovation can break barriers. While I’m grateful to share my own story, what’s even more powerful is seeing the collective wisdom and leadership of so many inspiring women. Their experiences remind us of what’s possible when we lift each other up and push boundaries together.

interactions.original_post:
Dear Bill Gates,

“Fifty years ago, you ignited a spark that became an unstoppable force for good. Your vision and relentless pursuit of possibility have not only revolutionized technology but have also touched lives accross the globe, including the remarkable individuals featured in this book. We stand on your shoulders, grateful for the path you’ve paved.”

This is an excerpt from The Women of Microsoft book we just wrote—coming out August 2025! 

We hope you love it! We hope the world does too! 

I can’t speak for all of us, but I speak for me, a first generation, non-technical immigrant from Venezuela turned learn-it-all and building Empressa AI on #Azure stack to empower women, Microsoft changed my life and my story. And I am so honored to share it with the world, along with all of the other women here.

I know it’s early for both dates, but at Microsoft we’re overachievers. Soooooo, 

Happy 50th Birthday, Microsoft and Happy #WomensHistoryMonth to all of you women making history!

To 50 more years—and beyond!

Love,
Miri

P.S. How does everyone like the cover? Do judge! 📖❤️💚💙💛

#MicrosoftLife #Microsoft50 #WomenInTech #MicrosoftWomen #Empressa #Latina #Mujer #IWD

After ten days of vacation,  I have some updates that I would like to share: I got laid off from Coinbase on June 30th. 

My employment was supposedly terminated with the previous round of layoff. Yet, there was an error in the process, so I received the notice weeks later than the other 18% of impacted employees. 

I want to thank Coinbase for the fantastic opportunity to work with many highly talented engineers in the field. I felt that my work impacted the day-to-day users on this platform. In addition, diving into the crypto world was eye-opening in breading my skills.

I felt lucky that I could take some time off as many of my friends who got impacted during this period had to change their plans due to immigration limitations. 

Lastly, I thank my family and friends for their support. If your company is Vancouver, Canada-based, or offers Canada remote work, I would love to learn more about it. I am looking for a mid-level backend engineer role. 

Thank you!

interactions.original_post:
🎓Officially Graduated! ✨ 

This August I graduated from two institutions: 
- University of Waterloo  with a Bachelor of Mathematics (major in Statistics, minor in CS)
- Wilfrid Laurier University  with a Bachelor of Business Administration (concentration in Marketing)

6 years, 2 degrees, 4 internships, and 2 startups. I am still processing how incredible yet challenging of a journey it has been. I am tremendously grateful for the amount of gracious support and generosity from my family, friends, professors, and colleagues I have been gifted to this point. After an hit-and-run accident in 2018, I reprioritized my life to focus on both my physical and mental health as well as what's meaningful to me. 

In the past two years, I completed research projects in Organizational Ambidexterity, Statistical Learning, Data-Intensive Distributed Computing and Computational Neuroscience. I also had the opportunity to work with various nonprofits to drive social impact in healthcare, human trafficking, and disability. I learned a ton about women and minority empowerment through Accelerate Her Future  Fellowship Circle

I am privileged to be given every opportunity to excel that others may not have. I am committed to continue driving transformative social impact in environmental sustainability, education, as well as women and minority empowerment (especially the Rights of Indigenous Peoples) through part-time studies and volunteering.

#UWaterlooProud #ForeverGolden

We've given away over 6,000 books in the last 3 weeks. 

A couple weeks ago, I posted here on LinkedIn about a book we've been giving out at SF AI meetups.

It's called Principles of Building AI Agents, and it's become popular among developers starting to build with AI, as well as their PMs and CEOs.

One engineer said that he started reading after a person he didn't know gave him one on a bus. 

A current YC founder called it "the most popular book in SF right now."

I expected to get a few dozen commenters. Instead, over 3,000 people have dropped us a line.

My LinkedIn DMs went crazy, and our team's been sending out copy after copy.

And if you want one, just comment "BOOK" and we'll send it over!

"My Precious!"

When my 13-year old's school vacation came around a couple of months ago, I did what every self-respecting helicoptering parent would do. I decided unilaterally that the best use of his time is to ensure that he does not suffer from the 2024 Oxford word of the year ("Brain rot"). That of course meant finding time away from contributing to upping the stock price of my company to focusing on my investment (my child) to increase my returns over time.

My son is an avid reader which I take the full genetic credit for. However, this time I chose to change the game and challenged him to attempt a different task to pass his time. We had a 500-piece jigsaw puzzle of Gollum, the puny creature who took it upon himself to keep Sauron single-eyed for nearly five hundred years.

With great enthusiasm comes a great start and a great one it was. He and I attacked the puzzle with gusto and with daily persistence got the outer boundaries of the puzzle going in a few days. The sessions were not drab. There was the Caravan Sa Re Ga Ma device playing old Hindi film songs as we powered through. And then followed the plateau. The pieces were hard to find. Everything looked like everything else. The colors and shades seemed to all blend into a hypnotic mess leading to headaches, fatigue and a general desire to leave it as is.

Just like in Lord of the rings, the days turned into weeks and the weeks into months. And Gollum lay there, quietly biding his time, mocking us with his smile since we had already put his face together. Flames of war were being lit around the puzzle. Our house help complained that the table could not be dusted because of the puzzle, our guests had to be given teacups directly in their hands since the puzzle dominated the table. 

Time became my enemy. I was Captain Ahab and the puzzle my whale. I chased it on weekends, five to ten pieces at a time. Strategies were employed to find similar pieces, I sought the help of my wife and son (who had long since moved on to brain rotting activities), Bangalore dust was repeatedly wiped off the pieces and the final pieces put together!

What did I take away from this exercise that makes it a good LinkedIn post? That persistence matters? That strategy matters? That you lead by example? All of the above, but the biggest takeaway was the one I should have known going into this enterprise - I need to get my eyesight checked!

#FridayMusings #Humour

I’m thrilled to share that I’ve accepted a position at Ford Motor Company as an Engine Powertrain Systems Engineer!

For me, Ford has always been more than just one of the most recognizable brands in the world — it’s a pioneer in innovation, sustainability, and community engagement. Growing up in India, the name Ford carried a sense of heritage and engineering excellence. 

Ever since moving to Michigan, I’ve been inspired by the company’s story — from creating the first moving assembly line to building icons like the Model T, Thunderbird, Mustang, and F-150.

To me, Ford represents the spirit of Detroit and, in many ways, the story of America itself. Joining this legacy feels both humbling and exciting — and I can’t wait to contribute to the next chapter.

I also want to take a moment to thank everyone who reached out after I was laid off earlier this year. Your kindness, referrals, and encouragement meant the world. I’ve learned firsthand that community isn’t just about connections — it’s about compassion. And I hope to pay that forward every chance I get.

For anyone currently job hunting, hang in there. I know how tough it can get when rejections pile up and your inbox is quieter than you’d like (except for that random JCPenney coupon). But there’s always another opportunity waiting — and the right one will find you when it’s meant to.
Here’s to new beginnings, old dreams, and the road ahead at Ford. #weareford

Excited to join the Hitachi Ventures team to lead AI and digital investments. 

I am incredibly grateful to the team at NGP Capital for their encouragement and mentorship over the years. I am looking forward to backing early-stage  founders building the next generation of infrastructure and platforms.

Losing my job after 7.5 years in automotive engineering was tough. But somethings aren’t in your control. This ends a role—not your journey.

Here’s what I’m doing to help me move forward:

•	I am pausing to reflect, not panic. Clarifying what excites me made job searching less overwhelming. I know managing product lifecycles, working with suppliers and launching high quality products is what I love doing. 

•	I am rebuilding my story, focusing on impact and transferable skills. Product management is a skill that’s transferable to any industry. I’m honing my brand and creating a my story that will resonate. 

•	I am investing in learning— Starting my Master’s in AI at Georgia Tech and completing certifications to stay future-ready

If you’re in this phase like me, remember: you’re not starting over, you’re starting from experience. 

Let’s connect—sometimes new beginnings lead to the best opportunities.

Onwards!

💼 After 16 incredible years at Microsoft… I'm moving on.
I’ve accepted a new role as Sr. Director of Engineering at Visa, reporting to the amazing Swati Shukla. [Under the inspiring Rajat Taneja and Harmeet Bala]

Microsoft was the launchpad I could only dream of as a tech-obsessed kid. Joining right after graduating from Manipal (before Satya made it cool 😉), I started as an SDET—getting paid to break Windows. I loved every minute.

A few years in, I realized I was drawn to shaping the what and why, not just the how. With support from [Krishnan Prasad, Anitha Bagyam, Vijay Mamtani, Abhishek Tiwari, Himanshu Soni & Satya Puniani], I transitioned to PM, moved from India to Redmond, and shifted from Networking to Security. Big changes, all at once.

In Windows Security, I worked on Smartcards, Biometrics, and Crypto, and helped lay the foundations of Windows Hello (shout out to Yogesh Mehta, Tony Ureche, Dave Bossio, Sunil Gottumukkala, Janani Vasudevan, Vijay Bharadwaj, Xiaohong Su, Nathan Ide and many more!). I then returned to Networking, leading work across VPN, Wi-Fi, DNS, and IoT, with incredible leaders and teammates (Abhishek Tiwari, Rajeev Nagar, Aaron Cunningham, Vineet Venugopal, Shai Guday, Ravi Rao, Lily Wang, Shahar ., Nikhil Gaekwad, Nick Wood and so many more! ). That phase included several patents (some in the photo below!).

Later, I joined Azure CXP, again working with [Billy Anders] and [Dino Natucci], where we built Community Support and Chat Support, saving Azure over $120M in modeled support costs.

🚀 PRIMO: My Final and Most Impactful Chapter
In PRIMO, with [Oren Rosenbloom, Angie Anderson, Swati Shukla, Sachin Trivedi, Dino Natucci, I helped lead:
★A unified CX Data Platform powering customer insights across Azure
★A widely adopted Observability solution used across engineering & field
★A bold reset of Azure Advisor to support resilient and optimized customer workloads

I’m especially proud of the PM team we built—some of the sharpest, most customer-obsessed minds I’ve worked with— also our engineering partners who brought heart, trust, and world-class execution as well as the amazing UX and Research team.

🔁 So why leave?
Because growth lives outside comfort.
After 16 years, it’s time to push myself in a new environment, stretch in new ways, and grow alongside a different set of challenges—especially as AI transforms how we build and lead.
I leave with deep gratitude to Billy Anders, Angie Anderson, Oren Rosenbloom, Dino Natucci, Sachin Trivedi, Salman Zafar,Matt Wagner 
, Saloni Singh and Sarah Ménard for their belief, mentorship, and support—and to the many teammates, friends, and mentors who shaped this journey.
Let’s stay connected 💙
 Onward and upward.

Instead of the Badge and Laptop, here is a different flavor showcasing the journey!

From Odisha to Microsoft: Celebrating Roots, Resilience & Community This AAPI Heritage Month

As we celebrate Asian and Pacific Islander (AAPI) Heritage Month, I find myself reflecting on a journey that began in a small town (Bhubaneswar) in Odisha, India, in a humble home filled with love, books, and the values of hard work and education. Raised in a teacher family, I learned early on that knowledge is a gift meant to be shared—and that with curiosity, resilience, and community, even the biggest dreams can find their wings.
 
Fast forward to today, I'm proud to be serving as a Principal Engineering Manager at Microsoft—a place where I get to build, lead, and learn every day, while staying deeply connected to who I am and where I come from.
 
Culture has always been my compass. Whether it was celebrating Diwali with homemade sweets and hand-painted diyas, or the simple joy of sharing stories in our native language over chai, these moments ground me. They taught me the power of belonging, and the richness that comes from honoring where we come from—while building something bigger together.
 
This month, I’m especially excited as Asian ERG SVC Bay Area Lead by the amazing work our community has done to bring people together to celebrate food, stories, traditions, and shared experiences. There’s so much beauty in our diversity, and so much power in our community.
 
💬 Let’s make this month a celebration of connection.
- What is a tradition, dish, or cultural value from your AAPI heritage that you enjoy sharing with others?
- Is there an AAPI leader, historical figure, or cultural icon who has inspired you? What about their story resonating with you?
- How has your understanding or appreciation of AAPI cultures evolved over time—either personally or professionally?
 
I’m incredibly grateful to be part of a community that leads with heart, purpose, and pride. Huge thanks to my fellow Asian ERG members Angela Rapadas , Paul Shin, Carolina Bogado Gomez, Vaishali Khandelwal, @Jie Li @Kim Lam and all the passionate individuals (Maha Govindarajan , @Priya Narang, Shorbani Sengupta, @Preeti Naik, @Swetha Joshi, @Mili Patel, Parvathy Krishnan, @Rachana Bhadravathi Shankarnaraya, @Meera Balsara) who make our events, conversations, and celebrations so meaningful. Your dedication, creativity, and commitment to fostering inclusion inspire me every day.

Special shoutout to Aanchal Gupta for her incredible energy, leadership and incredible support for Asian ERG community—you make our culture come alive! And heartfelt thanks to Angela Chen for her encouragement and support—it truly inspired me to share my story. Here's to continuing to uplift, support, and celebrate each other—not just this month, but every day. 💛

#AAPIHeritageMonth #AsianERG #MicrosoftLife #Microsoftbayarea

interactions.original_post:
Microsoft is celebrating an extraordinary milestone of 50 bold, groundbreaking, and unforgettable years! Across Microsoft locations worldwide, we are honoring the changemakers who don't merely envision the future but actively build it.

In my own 16 remarkable years at Microsoft, I've had the privilege of working across diverse consumer and enterprise offerings, embarking on thrilling 0-1 journeys, driving impactful growth initiatives, and securing US patents along the way. 

I feel immensely honored and humbled as Microsoft Silicon Valley spotlights my journey as part of the 50th Anniversary celebrations. Back in 2015, when I was based in Bellevue, Washington, I was part of an incredible team that created the first-ever prototype for Microsoft Teams during a spirited Hackathon project. Our vision was simple yet powerful: create a seamless digital workspace to unify people, conversations, and content, making teamwork effortless and efficient. Little did we know then that Teams would rapidly become one of Microsoft's fastest-growing applications in history. It emerged as a lifeline for millions of frontline workers, students, teachers, and colleagues worldwide, especially during the unprecedented COVID-19 pandemic. Today, Teams has evolved into an indispensable part of our daily lives. It continues to grow and now enriched with cutting-edge AI and virtual reality capabilities, further revolutionizing productivity and connection.

I invite you to watch this short video where I share some cherished memories of those early days while hacking at Microsoft. I’d love to hear your favorite Teams moment! #LifeatMicrosoft #Hackathon #MicrosoftTeams #MemoriesforLifeTime #Grateful

A reflection on 2024 

Even though as a recruiter I use LinkedIn on a daily basis, I have barely used it as a medium to connect with the community beyond some job related announcements. So changing that now in the hopes of connecting on a deeper level with the LinkedIn community!

To say that 2024 was like no other year would be an understatement to say the least and here’s why:

I started this year with a huge personal loss, lost my father on the very second day of this year, but I know that he’s in a better place and smiling down on me!

I also started the year by performing umrah (for those who know what it is) with my husband and children and it was truly a blessed experience for us as a family!

My son moved to a different state to start college and I am so proud of him for not just completing his first semester of undergrad with great grades but also dealing with so many things on his own and with so much maturity! So proud!!

On the professional side, had the amazing opportunity to expand my skills and work with a great team at HubSpot! Couldn’t have asked for better leadership and friendship than what I received from Ashley and my partners in all things good Martin and Steph. My journey at HubSpot would not have been the same without all the support from Sarah, Olivia, Melanie, Mark, Becky, Kaitlyn, James, Daphney, Alaina, Linda, Kaleigh,Sarah, Brandon and so many others that made each day so fulfilling for me!! So very thankful for everything and everyone that I got to know at Hubspot this and half of last year!

Starting a new job in the last month of the year is always interesting , now as I am back at Meta, I will admit that I am excited and a little nervous but mostly hopeful to continue the impact that I made three years ago 

Aside from my amazing colleagues and family, I am also so thankful for the friends, acquaintances and my larger LinkedIn community for being there for me over the years and for helping shape up so much of who I am today! I hope to continue to grow with all of you in the coming years!

Happy 2025!

A Lifetime Experience on the Family Fued Stage!

Last week, the show went live! I had the incredible opportunity to play in the Family Feud finale with Steve Harvey. While we didn’t take home the prize, the journey taught me lessons I’ll carry forever - both personally and professionally.

The experience of prepping as a team with my family was a masterclass in collaboration. We strategized, assigned roles, planned our responses, and most importantly, listened to each other. It reinforced how critical it is to think before reacting and how alignment on a shared goal can unlock a team’s true potential.

And then, there was the stage! Debuting on screen was surreal—walking under the bright lights, facing a live audience of 200 people, 5 cameras capturing every angle, and the magic of lights, camera, action. It truly felt like being in a celebrity moment with makeup artists and stylists helping us get ready for our big moment.

But the highlight? Meeting the legend himself, Steve Harvey. Hearing his journey from humble beginnings to stardom was a reminder that perseverance and resilience can lead to incredible heights. I even had the chance to share a bit about my work, my passion for travel, plans for our vineyard and events venue, and so much more. The cherry on top? Dancing with Steve while Harshita taught him some cool moves - it was unforgettable!

It is important to step out of your comfort zone, embrace new challenges, and find joy in the process. Celebrating the journey, not just the outcome is the key!

#Teamwork #FamilyFeud2024  #lifetimeExperience #lessonsLearned #growthMindset

Thrilled to share an important milestone in my PhD journey!
I successfully completed my PhD Defense!

It’s difficult to put into words what this journey has meant to me as years of challenges, growth, late nights, and continuous learning. This milestone is not just a personal achievement, but a reflection of the support, guidance, and collaboration I’ve been fortunate to receive throughout the process.

My deepest gratitude goes to my supervisor, Prof. Neeraj Goyal, for his constant support, patience, and belief in me. His guidance has shaped my research journey in more ways than I can express.

A sincere thanks to my DSC members Prof. V. N. A. Naikan, Prof. R. N. Rai, and Prof. Sudipta Mahapatra for their thoughtful feedback, questions, and encouragement throughout my PhD. Your insights have significantly strengthened my work.

I would also like to thank my external examiners, Prof. Mukesh Kumar Barua and Prof. Liudong Xing, for their valuable reviews, comments, and suggestions. Your inputs added great depth and clarity to my thesis and the defense discussion.

To all members of the Subir Chowdhury School of Quality and Reliability, IIT Kharagpur, thank you for the discussions, technical help, motivation, and most importantly, the camaraderie. This journey would have been much harder without your continuous support.

To my friends, colleagues, and family thank you for standing by me through every high and low. This PhD journey was never a solo effort, and I’m genuinely grateful to be surrounded by such supportive people. 

Excited for the next chapter ahead!
I am open to opportunities in industrial reliability roles as well as academic research positions.

#PhD #DefenceCompleted #ReliabilityEngineering #IITKharagpur #NetworkReliability

We are delighted to share that Dr. Arvind Tripathy - MBA, Ph.D. (Management), FDP (IIM Ahmedabad) has joined as the Dean of KIIT School of Management (KSOM), Bhubaneswar.

Prof. Arvind Tripathy is associated with School of Management since 2008. He has 25 years of experience consisting of 7 years of corporate experience and 18 years in academics. He was associated with the advertising industry in Mumbai and Bangalore and media services in IT training Kolkata. A Ph.D. in Management in the marketing area, he is an alumnus of the Department of Business Administration, Utkal University. He is an alumnus of IIM Ahmedabad (FDP program 2013).

Apart from his teaching assignments, he is actively involved in MDPs in organizations like SIDBI, IDBI Bank, Utkalika, OFSDP, Mahanadi Coal Fields and in-house training programs of All India Radio & Doordarshan. He has been a resource person/trainer in various marketing workshops of national and international fame - ‘Enhancing the capacity of middle and small scale enterprises in Orissa Regional Chapter’ was a European Union- Govt. of India project. 

He has served as a marketing consultant for ‘Capacity Enhancing’ program for the craftsman and handicrafts trading societies from Orissa organized by Trade Kraft UK and IRFT Mumbai. 

He is also involved in projects from Indian Council of Social Science Research, New Delhi (Ministry of MHRD, Govt. of India) in social sector as  Co-Project Investigator.

After an incredible 7+ years, my second stint at Microsoft has come to an end.

Microsoft has been more than just a workplace for me—it’s been a place of inspiration, growth, and deep admiration since the very beginning of my career. I’ve had the privilege of working alongside some of the brightest minds in tech, building products that touch millions, and learning what it means to create impact at scale. I’ll always be grateful for the people, the culture, and the opportunities that shaped me.
But now, it’s time for a new chapter.

I’ve always had the startup bug — and this time, I’m diving into a new space, Healthcare. I’m thrilled to share that I’ve co-founded  an AI-first therapy management platform purpose-built for Skilled Nursing Facilities (SNFs). Our mission is to fix documentation so clinicians can focus on what matters most: patient care.

The excitement is multi-fold—not just because of the technology, but because of the real-world impact we can create in a space that desperately needs innovation. We’re reimagining how therapy departments operate, with AI co-pilots, real-time compliance, and workflows that actually work for clinicians.

As I embark on this journey, I know I’ll need to lean on my network more than ever — for advice, support, connections, and encouragement. If you’re passionate about healthcare, AI, or just want to chat about building something meaningful from the ground up, I’d love to connect.

Here’s to bold beginnings, and to building something that matters. 🚀

#startupjourney #CareDocs #healthtech #AI #founderlife #gratitude #MicrosoftAlumni

R.I.P. The "Easy Apply" button. 🪦

Claude 4.5 Opus just made it obsolete.

I’ve stress-tested pretty much every AI model for writing cold outreach: GPT-4, Grok, Llama, the whole lineup.

They all fail the same way.

You can smell the AI from the first line.

“Hope you’re doing well…” 🤢

Then I tried Claude 4.5 and it was the first time an LLM didn’t sound like it was pretending to be human.

It actually understands professional dynamics.

Not just what to say, but why you’d say it.

I work closely with hiring teams, so I know exactly which messages get ignored and which ones spark real conversations. I spent ~5 hours dialing in a prompt that makes Claude 4.5 write networking DMs that consistently get responses.

What changed?
✔️ Messages that read like they came from a peer
 ✔️ Hooks grounded in real company context (no hallucinations)
 ✔️ Questions recruiters want to answer
 ✔️ Zero “please hire me” energy

The system does three things extremely well:
 → Breaks down the JD + your actual experience
 → Maps your skills to the team’s unspoken pain points
 → Crafts a “warm intro” that feels unmistakably human

I’m sharing the exact Claude 4.5 networking prompt plus a short guide on how to use it.

If you’re still relying on generic cover letters or hoping Easy Apply works someday… this is a much faster path to the top of the pile.

r.i.p. applying to 100 jobs a week.

AI just made it obsolete.

i've tested every job search strategy. mass applications, networking, recruiter outreach, resume optimization...

none of it led to reliable interviews.

then i built an AI that fixes job search from the ground up.

it's called poozle, and it uses machine learning to pre-qualify you for roles before you waste time applying.

here's how it works:

most job boards show you everything and make you guess which ones are worth your time.

poozle's AI does the opposite: 
→ it analyzes your background (experience, skills, career trajectory) 
→ it scans 100,000+ job postings in real-time 
→ it calculates your statistical match score for each role 
→ it only shows you jobs where you're likely to land an interview

then it does something no other platform does: it caps applicant pools at 50.

when the AI shows you a role, you're guaranteed to be one of the first 50 applicants. not applicant 487.

the result: you apply to 10 jobs instead of 100. and you get 4x more interviews.

here's what makes the AI different:
→ pre-qualification matching: it doesn't just keyword match. it understands your actual experience and maps it to company needs 
→ real-time applicant tracking: it knows when a role hits 50 applicants and stops showing it 
→ freshness ranking: it prioritizes jobs that are actively scheduling interviews 
→ swipe interface: find a match? swipe right. bad fit? swipe left. the AI learns from every decision.

job seekers are using this to escape the "easy apply" black hole.

here's what the difference looks like:

without the AI (standard job boards): 
→ scroll through 1,000s of jobs manually 
→ guess which ones are worth applying to 
→ spend 20 min per application 
→ compete with 800+ people 
→ get ghosted 95% of the time

with the AI (poozle): 
→ open the app 
→ AI shows your top pre-qualified matches 
→ swipe right on 7 of them (takes 30 seconds) 
→ you're applicant number 12, 8, and 23 
→ interview requests within a week

same resume. same skills. different AI.

the job search isn't a human problem anymore. it's an AI problem.

and most people are still using 2015 tools.

i'm giving away free access to the AI.

if you're tired of applying to black holes, this is your shortcut.

comment "POOZLE" below (make sure we're connected so i can DM you) and i'll send you the download link + free pro access.

available on ios + android.

p.s. the AI works best when you let it learn your preferences. swipe on 20-30 jobs and it gets scary good at showing you perfect matches.

Most people are still prompting GPT-5 like it’s GPT-4.

That’s why they’re only getting average answers.

Here's the big shift that happened:

ChatGPT-3.5/4: You could type one vague line and get something decent.
ChatGPT-5: It demands structure and rewards you with smarter, more reliable results.

The 6-Part GPT-5 Prompt Formula by Open AI

➤ Role – Tell it who to be
➤ Task – Define the exact outcome you want
➤ Context – Give background + constraints
➤ Reasoning – Ask it to explain / validate answers
➤ Output Format – Specify tables, bullets, steps, etc.
➤ Stop Conditions – Set when the task is complete


GPT-5 doesn’t just “guess a reply.”
It:
➤ Thinks step-by-step before answering
➤ Uses context much better
➤ Produces cleaner, structured outputs
➤ Saves you from endless back-and-forth edits

4. Example:
Act as a travel guide. 
Find 3 lesser-known hikes within 2 hours of San Francisco. 
Exclude tourist hotspots. 
Cross-check with credible sources. 
Return results in a Markdown table: Hike name | Address | Time | Summary. 
Stop when 3 verified hikes are listed.

If you want average, prompt GPT-5 like GPT-4.
If you want next-level answers, use the 6-part structure.

Building with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is more than just picking an LLM — it’s about designing a modular, scalable, and future-ready tech stack.

⚙️ Here's a quick breakdown of the modern RAG stack (credit: Kalyan KS):

🔹 LLMs (Open vs Closed)
 → Open: LLaMA 3, Phi-4, Mistral (⚙️ control, customization)
 → Closed: OpenAI, Claude, Gemini (🚀 performance, simplicity)
 🎯 Tradeoff: Flexibility vs Convenience

🔹 Frameworks
 → LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack, txtai — the glue for chaining, memory, routing, & tool-use logic.

🔹 Vector Databases
 → Chroma, Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus
 ⚡ Powering fast, relevant, scalable retrieval.

🔹 Data Extraction (Web + Docs)
 → Crawl4AI, FireCrawl, LlamaParse, Docling
 📥 Raw data = richer context = better answers.

🔹 Open LLM Access Platforms
 → Hugging Face, Ollama, Groq, Together AI
 🔧 Simplifying infra & accelerating experimentation.

🔹 Text Embeddings
 → Open: Nomic, SBERT, BGE
 → Proprietary: OpenAI, Google, Cohere
 📐 Retrieval quality starts here.

🔹 Evaluation
 → Ragas, Trulens, Giskard
 🔍 Measure hallucination, grounding, relevance, and model behavior.

🧠 𝗧𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗮𝘄𝗮𝘆:
 RAG ≠ integration problem. RAG = design problem.

 Every layer affects latency, explainability, quality, and cost.

💬 What does your #RAG stack look like today?

I’m happy to share that I’m starting a new position as Software Development Engineer at Amazon Web Services (AWS)!

This week, Friday, was my last day of employment at Microsoft.

I am leaving the company to try something different... both in terms of the domain and the size of the company. Although I am super excited for the adventure and experience coming along my way, I do have mixed feelings, as well, because Microsoft has helped me grow tremendously, both in my career and as a person in general, with its culture built on the following pillars.

**Customer Obsession.** We always listen to our customers and try to understand their needs, and we prioritize our work accordingly. We create impact by not only shipping features that directly meet our customers' needs but also contributing to others' success via, e.g., open-source projects and conferences. During my tenure, I have had the privilege and honor to lead two important projects, among others, both of which got presented by Satya Nadella himself in his keynotes at Microsoft Build conferences. Likewise, I have had the opportunity to contribute to open-source projects and present our work in different international PostgreSQL conferences and events in the US and Europe.

**Growth Mindset, Diverse and Inclusive, One Microsoft.** For me, these pillars of the Microsoft culture have been the most transformational. Coming from a non-CS background, I managed to be part of the Azure Database for PostgreSQL team. Thanks to the support of our leadership team, I was admitted to Azure Data University, an internal program to help engineers upskill themselves in the state-of-the-art database research and technologies, and upon successfully graduating from the program, I had the privilege to participate as an assistant in the next cohort of the program. Similarly, my managers also provided great support (and flexibility when I needed) for me to obtain an executive MBA degree during my tenure.

Of course, it was not only roses. There were a lot of times when I needed to put my perseverance and resilience to the test. In the end, however, I believe I have become a stronger professional and individual.

As I depart from Microsoft, I would like to thank, once again, all my former and current managers, the leadership team, our product managers and all my colleagues for providing such a great and supportive environment.

#MicrosoftAlumni

interactions.original_post:
I have been part of more than 1000+ interviews (500+ at amazon and ~500 in different settings) 
I'm going to share some of the most important things which I learned from these interviews. 

🧩 Crack the System Design Interview — Part 1 of 20

What Interviewers Are Actually Scoring You On 
Most engineers walk into a system design interview believing one thing:
"If I get the architecture right, I pass."
I used to think the same thing - that belief alone has cost some very talented engineers the job.
Here's the truth nobody tells you upfront.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The interview isn't about the perfect design.
There is no perfect design. Every system has trade-offs. Every architecture decision you make solves one problem and creates another. Interviewers know this. What they're really watching is how you think, not what you know.
I've seen engineers sketch a flawless distributed system and still fail. I've also seen engineers with a messy whiteboard get the offer. The difference wasn't talent. It was how they communicated.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
 Here's what's actually being evaluated:
- Problem Clarity Do you ask the right questions before jumping in? Or do you assume you already know what needs to be built?
- Structured Thinking Is your approach organized and easy to follow? Can the interviewer see where you're going?
- Trade-off Awareness Do you acknowledge what you're giving up with each decision? Every choice has a cost. Senior engineers name it.
- Communication Can you explain your thinking clearly while you're still figuring things out? This one is underrated and it matters enormously.
- Adaptability When the interviewer pushes back or changes a constraint, do you adapt — or do you freeze?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The pattern I see most often:
An engineer spends 40 minutes building a beautiful system. Never once explains why they made a choice. Never asks a clarifying question. And when the interviewer says "what if we needed 10x the traffic?" — they fall apart.

That's not a knowledge problem. That's a communication problem.

The best candidates I've seen treat the interview like a live problem-solving session with a colleague. They think out loud. They say things like "I'm going with a relational database here because consistency matters more than scale for this use case — but I'd revisit that if we hit 50 million users." That one sentence tells me more than a perfect diagram ever could.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The three questions every interviewer is quietly asking themselves:
❓ Would I want to work through a hard problem with this person? 
❓ Do they stay clear and calm under pressure? 
❓ Do they know what they don't know — and are they honest about it?

If you can make an interviewer answer yes to all three, the technical details almost take care of themselves.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
#SystemDesign
